description:
In which Pooh and his friends race through the 100 Aker Wood, collecting honeypots as they go!

This game is a dice rolling game, in which players take turns to roll dice, and move their characters along the spaces. As they travel through the game board they will collect honeypots. But beware! Sometimes you might lose a honeypot.

It's not a race to the finish. The winner is the player with the most honeypots at the end of the game.

Based on the original E.H.Shepard illustrations for Winnie the Pooh books.

Made in England.

boardgame: Caves of Rwenzori: The Intrepid Expansion
description: The Intrepid expansion grows the core game into a 6 player game or enlarges your existing game. Included in the box are x20 Cave Tiles, a purple meeple, a magnificently-designed new character (the Colonel) and a new Special Gear Card. Adventurer, gather your wits!

Taumant's Lair is a dice game for a single player with a duration of approximately 20 minutes. To play you need six six-sided dice, each not exceeding 0.5 inches, about eight pawns, and a dozen counters or tokens. If you do not have any pawns nor counters.
The game is divided into three different parts, each corresponding to three pages, which you will use as game boards. Print each one on a different sheet of cardboard and follow their instructions.

Part I: Recruiting Adventurers
The first step to penetrate Taumant's lair is to form a party capable of surviving its many hazards. Luckily, you are near to the city-state of Kalazar, located between two kingdoms at war. It makes Kalazar the perfect place to look for adventurers. You must explore the city and try to recruit people prepared to risk their lives.

Part II: The Dungeon
It's the time! Your party is about to get into the cavern. Your goal is to get through its six levels with as few losses as possible. You will play six turns, each corresponding one level of the cave. Put the pawns representing your adventurers on the appropriate square of the column 'ready' and another one on the first level of the dungeon. If you beat the sixth level, you will face Taumant in Part III with your surviving adventurers!

Part III: Taumant!
You have arrived at Taumant's chamber. This will be a tough fight! As soon as he meets the adventurers, he prepares himself to reduce his enemies to ashes with his deadly fire breath. You have only three turns before he buries your party in a hell of flames. Every unwounded adventurer has a roll for every turn. Put this sheet 12 inches from you and roll a die towards the dragon; if it stops on him, you will cause damage points depending on its location. If you cause 50 or more damage points, you win! Otherwise, you can return to Part I and try again.

—description from the designer

The jung pirates going on a treasure hunt using a secret treasure map. They have to find out how to avoid danger and get quickest to the final. This game is training memory and strategic thinking. 
The main goal is to reach the pirate beach with the treasure. This can be archived only if they roll the exact numbers on the dice.

—user summary

As a Free Captain of the Rim you roam the far reaches of space as a trader and mercenary. Secretly though you are an agent of the Intergalactic Council sent on a clandestine mission to explore planets infiltrated by three alien races known as The Cosmic Hegemony who plan to invade the peaceful worlds of Intergalactic Alliance.

Find all their world shattering Super Weapons, using your Combat Skills, Diplomacy, and Super Science, and stop them before it is too late!

The game uses a bag builder mechanic to add and remove dice from a bag. Cards are played to explore various sectors of space and 6 custom dice are used for combat situations.

Pulp Invasion X1 expands the worlds of Pulp sci-fi space adventure novels with more adventurers to explore the new planets with and new alien allies to confront, plus expands the base game by adding a variant for 2 players.

—description from the publisher

description:
Oh, Whelp! is a communist-style card drafting game. You play as a dragon and guide them on their journey of self-development. Three Companion cards are drawn each round, each of which offer strengths and weaknesses to your dragon's personality.

Players simultaneously choose the Companion best suited for their dragon's character goals. Points are marked along their own individual trait tracker. The Companion card deck is played through twice (16 rounds). Personality points are tallied and the dragon with the highest-scoring personality wins!

This light party game is an ideal opener at game nights or when playing with folks who might be inexperienced with heavier games. The multiplayer solitaire format easily allows for solo play and up to an unlimited number of players. Oh, Whelp! is a free game to print and play and can be played virtually, making it highly accessible to all. Did we mention it is heavily infused with puns?

Lost Temples of Gold is a Solitaire Roll & Write game where you are an explorer that has been commissioned by the king to sail to distant lands, explore the terrain and bring back Diamonds, Gems and Artifacts. You will have a set number of Crew and Soldiers to explore but you better leave some crew and soldiers behind to protect the ship from the hostile natives. As you explore the three Islands, you will develop a map to help you along the way. You will also need to collect or buy wood to build a raft so you can cross to the next island. Two of the Islands have Tribal Chiefs that are hidden and you must find. These Chiefs will each give you half of the secret location of the temple.

The Chief will either greet you with hostility or friendship depending on how you treat his people. As you explore you will come across different types of villages that you can trade with or overtake if necessary. Some will be hostile and automatically attack you while others will be friendly. Keep track of your food because if you run out your crew will start to die from starvation. Try to obtain medicine in case your crew get sick from the natives. Once you've spoke with both Chiefs you will have the secret location and can travel to the last island. Search the island and find the Temple to set your name in history and your King the richest in the nation. If you lose all your crew you lose the game.

—description from the designer

Suddenly, a portal opens up and someone walks through it - not just anyone, but you. Again.

Before you can say anything, the other you says, “Shut up and listen. This is a time machine. You know what you have to do.” As you are handed the machine, your mind immediately goes back to that one event that you always wished that you could have changed.

This is your chance to change it. You see on the readout that the machine is already primed to go to the exact date and time that you were thinking of. As you press the activate button, the other you says, “Don’t screw it up like I did.”

The Second Most Perfect Moment is a standalone (and combinable) expansion to The Perfect Moment. It's made up of 3 small expansions that were released in 2019.

Players race to rewrite history in their favor using a deck of only 18 cards. Players use equipment, exploit paradoxes in the time-space continuum, and author their own revision of the past. Featuring a clever multi-sided card system, every card is used by both players based on what they can see. Equipping that card for yourself may just give your opponent the ability they were looking for. Every turn, players activate up to two revealed cards, which each have special effects. After the effects are resolved, the player can optionally score a card to earn up to 3 points depending on how closely they match it. The first player to 16 points wins. It takes careful planning and a bit of luck to avoid failing like you have so many times before. This time will be different.

This time...


This can be played on its own or combined with The Perfect Moment and expansions.

Includes 3 previously released expansion sets of the series, and the solo mode rule set:

     1979: A Job Interview Gone Wrong
     1987: A Little Espionage Gone Wrong
     2010: An Escape Plan Gone Wrong
     The Perfect Moment: Echoes (2nd version)

Created by The Kindness Collective, Listen Up is a conversational board game that tests your listening skills and inspires deeper connections between friends and family! Packaged into a compact and easy-to-play game with over 250 questions and activities, ranging from deep and meaningful to light-hearted and fun, Listen Up is guaranteed to reveal untold stories, heartfelt moments, and plenty of laughs with those closest to you!

The game is simple. Each player moves around the board answering personal questions according to the category they land on. Other players can delve deeper into their answers by using GET REAL cards, and make sure to remember their responses to gain extra points at the end!

—description from the publisher

Superhit is a set of many games designed for education in arithmetic. The common components are dice, counters in five colors, and a 10x10 board with numbered spaces; the numbers also have various colors.

Some of the later games are suitable for play by adults. In one, players roll four dice and then must use arithmetic operations to produce a number on the board, which they place a counter on. When certain dice combinations are rolled the player with the top counter on a space may move the stack. The object is generally to reach the edge of the board, but (as with all the games in the set) there are many variants and optional rules.

Counteract is played on a 6x6 board with the squares numbered from 1-6; one space of each number is a "magic square" with a star on it. Each player has a supply of counters.

On a typical turn a player rolls a die and places a counter on an empty space with the matching number. They may take another turn if a 6 is rolled. Each player may make a "power play" three times during the game; this involves rolling two dice and allows replacing an opposing piece at the designated coordinates if there is one.

The game ends when one player uses all their pieces. Scoring is primarily based on forming lines of six pieces and on occupying the magic squares, although there are some extra points available to the player who goes out.

description:
After aiding the downed Liberation ships, the Tempus follows them into space, her home planet no longer safe. A nearby moon has been chosen for the Liberation's new headquarters. These ships are loaded with supplies and crew to build it, but any help would be appreciated.

Games in the Tempus series work similarly to roll and write games where players roll dice and use the results to fill in a sheet. In the Tempus System, instead of using dice, players will use the date and time to randomize setup and available actions.

A dicey game about getting in and getting out.

Inspired in the cult film Ocean's  Eleven.


The Big Score is a competitive craps-like dice rolling game about attempting to rob a casino and get out first.

The game is played over multiple rounds, with each round having the players take turns trying to move their crew to the vault, or out past the lobby, while the non-active players place bets on what they will roll, trying to get a bonus advancement for their own crew.


The Big Score is the postcard game from Button Shy's April 2016 edition of the Board Game of the Month Club.

Get knocked down, but get up again in this barroom brawl.

Inspired on the one hit wonder song Tubthumping by Chumbawamba.

Chip Beauvais and Sara Beauvais team up again for the third(?) Button Shy project, and I imagine there are more to come too. In Thubtumping, players will try to get one of each drink (coin) by fistbumping a player that has a smaller coin than you. Being realtime, it can feel like a barroom brawl, and with all of those fists flying, it could accidentally turn into one.

—description from the publisher


Tubthumping is a simultaneous play competitive game of drinking and brawling. Players supply coins or tokens to play, and attempt to win mini-brawls with each other in order to collect one coin or token of each color. The first one to do so wins.


Tubthumping is the postcard game from Button Shy's June 2017 edition of the Board Game of the Month Club.

description: Your goal is simple: launch your goat, land it on the cliffs and try to be the last one standing. Ready, set, GOAT! In this simple-to-learn children's game, players spin the spinner and move their trolleys around the board, attempting to visit each of the landmarks and neighbors in the Neighborhood of Make-Believe: Grandpere at the Eiffel Tower, King Friday and Queen Sara at the castle, Corny at the Rock-It Factory, X & Henrietta Pussycat at the tree, Lady Elaine at the Museum-Go-Round, Dr. Bill and Elsie Jean at the platypus mound, and Daniel Tiger at the clock.

Along the way, Mister Rogers cards offer bonuses and penalties (often with a motivational thought to soften the blow of the penalties). Landing on a Trolley space offers an extra turn.

The game offers three methods of play:
1. Players must circle the board and collect all seven of the Neighbor cards by landing at the colored landmarks for each one.
2. Players start with all seven cards and must "drop off" each Neighbor at their landmarks.
3. The "Advanced" game, in which players must collect all seven neighbor cards, and once all seven are collected, drop them off.

The game box suggests a minimum age of 6 years old, but the game is about on par with something like Candy Land. Ages 4 and 5 can learn and play it.

description:
Shingo is a set collecting game wherein 2-6 players try to score points by adding counters to a playmat that correspond to the same symbol and location on cards that they build a hand of during the game.

The game starts with a stack of cards being placed face down in the middle of the table, and three cards being peeled off and laid face up adjacent to the stack.

On each player's turn they select a card from the three face up ones and add it to their hand, and after doing so add a counter or counters to their playmat, according to whether they have a pink star in that location on the card they've picked up. If they have stars in multiple locations on the card, they add a counter to each of those locations on their playmat, whether or not they have a counter there already from a previous turn, and regardless of whether or not there is a nought or a cross in that location on another card.

Once every player has a hand of seven cards, the game ends and the players add up their points. For more info on scoring and other points, please follow the link to the rules file.

A free PNP game for one player. You are the leader of a flock of Geese heading south for the winter. Lead your flock through the Hunters and avoid the lurking Eagles to reach the safety of the Marshlands.

You have six Geese that you must guide to safety by moving them each turn towards the Marshlands at the bottom of the gameboard. In your path are six Hunters who try to shoot you and eight Eagles who try to eat you. Each turn, you use dice rolls as an AI to determine their movements. If they reach any of your Geese, that bird is captured and removed from the board.

You win if at least one of your Geese reach safety in the Marshlands by successfully passing through the Hunters and Eagles. Variable levels of difficulty can be set by adjusting the number of Eagles on the board.

The board game bulls-bears about the stockpile. In the game you have to buy and sell stocks. The one who buys more than 50% of the shares wins. This is a board game with website interface. Website of the game is needed for make game mechanics easier.

At the beginning of the game all players have the same amount of money and shares. Player can buy or sell min 1 and max 9 shares on the turn. The share price changes depending on the players' decision. If more shares were sold than purchased, the price falls; if the opposite is true, the price rises.

The game ends when one of the participants buys more than 50 shares or becomes bankrupt.

TRIAMAND. Somewhere in a remote desert, an ancient tomb has been disturbed. An evil spirit has woken and is threatening to the whole world with an act of revenge. 
  You as a player need to save the world. The world counts on you! You must put bricks back on a tomb. Do it first, and you are the winner!!! 
  Game is played by 2 – 6 people, ages 7+. At the start, each player is given 5 playing cards, for which can be acquired bricks from the Brickbank. The bricks have different shapes and correspond to the color of the card. Amount paid for each brick can be the same as the price or higher. If brick is bought for its stated nominal value, a player can make an additional move. If paid by the nominal, it can be bought as many bricks as laying on the Brickbank (not from the treasury storage). After the player’s turn is over, every time empty brick spots on the Brickbank must be covered by new bricks.
  First move on the game is given to the player with highest value card. If several players have similar highest cards, they are comparing their next highest cards until someone has an upper hand.  
  On players turn, can be made one of the following:
-    Card drawn from the stock
-    Brick bought from the Brickbank
-    Brick removed /replaced. * if a row is completed, no brick can be removed or replaced.  
  Seems too easy? Not so fast, my friend. Competition is quite hard, and players need to come up with some strategy in order to succeed. Here are some hurdles: 
-    Although some bricks stretch up to the next rows, which is acceptable, player cannot build next row without completely bricklaying preceding one.
-    You have laid almost all bricks and now have stuck for some particular piece: don’t have necessary currency to buy this exact brick? Think strategy again: maybe you don’t need to cover up row too fast before you acquire necessary resources from the Brickbank. You need to be good at reading patterns. 
-    First move always starts from the left bottom corner marked with red. Next moves can be done freely, but within the same row until it is filled and ready to move to the upper one.  
 Good luck!!!
  Notice: Game is claimed strategic, and it truly is. In order to win, players must plan their movements in advance. The higher up the pyramid player climbs, the fewer choices one has and has to be very careful. Game is well suitable for school children to develop strategic thinking, to learn better geometrical shapes, and learn to count. Game is fun for adults also and is not burdening with the time required- just about 30 minutes to play one round. Quite addictive: lost players want a replay to get the satisfaction.

—description from the designer

Air battles rules for the 1960-1985 period

For 1/300th miniatures.

“My God! What will the Country say?"

-- President A. Lincoln

The Chancellorsville campaign, which took place in and around Virginia’s Wilderness in May 1863, is considered by many to be Robert E. Lee’s masterpiece, a true triumph “against the odds.” Opposing Lee was Union commander Joseph “Fighting Joe” Hooker, who had devised a plan that seemed assured of success. His cavalry would raid deep behind Confederate lines, cutting Lee’s supplies, while Hooker and four of his seven infantry corps would march west, then south, and appear behind the Confederate defenses opposite the city of Fredericksburg.

Lee would be compelled to withdraw south, in which case he would be pursued, or he would be compelled to attack with his numerically inferior army to avoid being crushed between the hammer and anvil of Hooker’s forces. “May God have mercy on General Lee, for I will have none,” Hooker declared.

No plan, however, survives first contact with enemy. With the advantage of interior lines and a great deal of audacity Lee would confound his opponent by continually bringing more rifles to the critical place at the critical time. And it didn’t’ hurt that Lee’s largest corps was commanded by one “Stonewall” Jackson. . . .

Lee’s Greatest Victory is an area-impulse game but one unlike anything seen before. Since the appearance of the “sunset die roll” nearly three decades ago players have alternated activating a single area, with variation coming in the form of an uncertain turn length. Play has a chess-like feel, with players able to act and react with a great deal of prior calculation. Short turns could be frustrating for one or both players. In this revolutionary development of the genre players no longer know how many impulses they, or their opponent, will have, creating both opportunities and crises that cannot be anticipated in advance—just like real combat. The “momentum” swings back and forth but rarely the same way twice. At the same time, each turn lasts as long as the players wish it to last, ensuring something will happen every turn.

Units of maneuver are Union divisions and Confederate brigades for the most part, with historical leaders present to provide a combat boost though at the risk of being wounded or KIA. Pontoon bridges and entrenchments are part of the basic rules while optional rules incorporate weather and optional units for both sides. Best of all, it's just six turns long and very playable in one sitting.

Content:

    Map - One full color 22"x34" mapsheet
    Counters - 176 full color 5/8" die-cut pieces
    Rules length - 12 pages
    Charts and tables - 2 pages
    Complexity - Medium
    Playing time - Up to 3 hours
    How challenging is it solitaire? - Poor


Design - Michael Rinella
Development - Paul Rohrbaugh
Graphics - Mark Mahaffey

Welcome to Sparkle*Kingdom!

If you are just joining the Sparkle*Kitty series, you are in luck! This 100% FREE version was created to allow families and friends to enjoy a silly spell slinging game whether you are trapped together in towers or spread apart in different kingdoms. It’s a bit different from the base Sparkle*Kitty card game, but with the same adorable giggle jiggles, otter devastations and added coloring fun!

Players use a Sparkle deck from previous games (or online) to fill in their colors in their kingdom. The player with the highest score wins.

Inspired by the video game TETRIS, except you play this version with the use of dice. Each turn you roll 6 colored dice and place them on the drop zone. Instead of having the shapes fall, you will be trying to arrange the dice to fall and make the shapes.  There are five colored dice that will be used to match all the similar colored shapes.

As each new shape is discovered, you will be trying to match the dice to these shapes before they move off the board and generate a new shape.  Each shape you match with the dice causes the dice to be removed from the board and the other dice to fall down into their place.

You start the 1 minute timer. You can use six actions to manipulate the dice to help you line them up and create stacks to generate the shapes. If you run out of time, the dice fall as they are.

Try to create as many shapes as possible to collect the highest score.  Be careful with your actions because if you leave too many dice on the board, they may start to stack too high and cause you to lose the game.

Celebrate the diversity of tea cultures in Chai: High Tea, an expansion for Chai! Merchants spice up their teahouses with new player powers, featuring afternoon tea to refreshing horchata, or a sip of yerba mate.

Tea merchants receive unique double-sided ability boards at the start of the game. Abilities are used as an optional free action on a turn, and the board flips over whenever an ability is used.

This expansion includes 6 ability boards with 12 unique abilities, in addition to 2 transparent dice. Additional rules make the expansion compatible with Chai’s solo and co-op gameplay.

-description from publisher

Each player has a pawn and travels on the board showing a route through the (Western) German wine cultivation regions according to the roll of a die. If you roll a 1 or 2, you may draw a card and follow the instruction or answer the question instead of advancing your pawn. For a correct answer you get a wine box tile (that may contain from 6 to 30 bottles). If a player reaches the last space, the game ends and the player with the most bottles wins.

This game was produced by Otto Maier Verlag, Ravensburg, Industrie- und Promotion-Service as a commercial game.

—user summary

Quarantine is a cooperative board game where players are quarantined at home, fending off a virus, conserving their food and trying to find a cure.

Each player takes on the role of a family member, who all have different character traits for how much they can move and carry, which are all useful to be successful in the game. They must keep socially distanced from one another (two spaces) while moving around the house, avoiding the virus tokens which replicate as the game progresses, and collecting the item tokens needed to make a cure. Each player can carry 10 food tokens (25 for Granny) and these are exchanged for movement and can be restocked from the kitchen but the supply is limited so be careful, if the food supply runs out or someone catches the virus the game is over.

Each turn consists of movement and/or an action (restock food, disinfect spaces, mix cure items or knock down walls to escape the virus). Movement is dictated by the number of food a player wishes and each player has a number of spaces that their character is permitted to move per food.

After each turn players draw a card from the research deck, this identifies either a new item or new virus in the house or gives the player a powerup to use later (extra moves, search the deck, haz-mat suit for temporary virus immunity). Following a round (one go for each player) all the viruses on the board are moved one space closer to the nearest player. Most viruses appear outside the house and need to break in, giving players time to prepare, but occasionally new viruses appear inside the house and urgent disinfection is required.

The mechanic for placing viruses involves a D12, with two faces for internal placement and 10 for external. New items from the research deck are placed with a D20 (room selection) and a D6 (space selection). Dice are not involved in player movement or decision outcomes.

The game ends when either a player runs out of food, a player succumbs to the virus or the parts of the cure are mixed and the cure is completed.

Carcassonne: Halb so wild is a mini-expansion consisting of 24 triangular landscape tiles that are half the size of a normal Carcassonne  tile. Each player receives 2 triangular tiles during setup, & can place them during their turns instead of drawing & placing a normal tile.

This set combines Halb so wild I & Halb so wild II, with updated art matching the 2.0 and 3.0 versions of the base game. Both mini-expansions are now bundled & sold together by Hans im Glück — originally Halb so wild I was only available through Spielbox. Part of the Carcassonne series.

Create and cultivate your own magical garden. Each turn, you will be placing tiles to produce crops and harvest magic beans, enchanted apples and cursed pumpkins. Hire and place gnomes, elves and trolls to harvest even more crops or to spoil your opponents production. The first player to harvest 10 crops of the same type wins the game!

—description from the publisher

Lords of Florence recreates the battle for political control of the Republic of Florence in the time of the Renaissance, a period famous for bankers, spectacular art, literature, betrayal and bloody conflict.Players control powerful families and individuals who together ruled the Republic during its 300 year history, as members of the Signoria (the ruling council of Florence), together they compete to gain enough political power (VPs) to overthrow the Republic and be named Duke.

Over 6 turns, players race to earn the most political power (VPs), if any player earns 50 political power, at the end of any turn before the end of turn 6, they automatically win.

In a turn, players spend their resources to complete shared projects, which in turn provide resources and VPs when completed. Projects are expensive, so players are encouraged to cooperate to complete them; yet the assymetric player powers mean some projects are more beneficial for some players than others. Therefore players must consider carefully whether to cooperate or not. In addition, each turn players may blind bid for intrigue cards, which provide opportunities to attack other players, disrupt projects they have completed, steal resources or even provide alternative victory conditions. However, players who focus too much on attacking others will not only fail to complete projects that would benefit them, but also risk triggering the shared loss condition. Failing to complete projects comes with a price - increasing the threat of the shared loss condition (known as instability), as the people of Florence become angry that their demands to complete projects are disregarded. If instability rises too far, the players all lose, forcing players to cooperatively complete at least some projects every turn to avoid defeat.

Player resources and intrigue cards are hidden behind player shields, allowing players to bluff, extort and hide their true strategies. The game rewards careful planning, timing and ruthlessness – all qualities critical to political success in the cutthroat World of Renaissance Italy

—description from the publisher

Around Australia Car Trial is a three-dimensional game of skill to navigate a car similar to a FX Holden around a winding course in Australia.

Published in the mid to late 1950s. The colorful board has a map of Australia overlaid by a road that begins near Melbourne and finishes near Perth. Interesting the game board displays several Mobil gas stations with the red Pegasus logo.

The plastic car is steered along the path using a magnetic control device, situated under the board. It passes through plastic tunnels along its travels. Points are scored by passing cities or time trials can be played. The player with the most points or fastest time is the winner.

The game is similar to Tarco games produced in the US around about the same time period and published by Sidney A Tarrson Co.

The Two Page Rules series features rules that are 2 pages long with one combat card that has the information needed to play the game. The sequence of movement is decided by a dice roll and after that, the games follow the sequence of move-fire-move-fire. Casualties are decided simply and effectively. Morale is divided into 4 stages with the chance to rally at each stage. New players should be able to conduct their own transactions by the third move. These are combat rules that reflect the tactics and technologies of their era. Decisions are made by players, not cards or dice. These rules can be used for nearly any scale of the figure. They are great for large or small battles.

description:
Hey, thanks for picking up a shift or two! It's been busy; guests have been coming at all hours of the day and night to warm their frozen feet and fill up on fries, burgers, and our famous fruit pies. As you can see there are no tables, just the row of barstools there. Guests are everyone's responsibility. We don't split tips here - the person who's served a guest the most food will get everything. Pay attention to what they like, and they'll tip well. Well, I think that's it... go talk to Gus in the back and he'll get you an apron and paper hat. Welcome aboard!

In The Lucky Penny Diner players are the waitstaff in a 24-hour diner, serving orders to guests, and trying to collect the most in tips. Cards are drafted into players' hands, then served to guests by laying the cards in an overlapping fashion beside them, representing how full they are getting.

What you serve, when, and to whom factor into how well you are rewarded. Do you serve dessert to a guest that your opponent is waiting on so that they can't serve them any more apps or entrees? Do you give it to the guy at the end to turn his two courses into three? Or do you serve it to the girl with the sweet tooth who will tip more for it?

—description from the designer

Putting the ‘reality’ back into your fantasy games

Welcome to Undying Lands a completely revised version of FLD. The constant evolution of the rules is thanks to the feedback I have received. Before I briefly cover the new features of the rules I would like to firstly focus on why these rules came about. The purpose and intent of this game is to provide players with a much more realistic fantasy game in contrast to the artificially  contrived contemporary games that pay little attention to deployment, command control, light troops, orders, linear tactics and ignore the problems of movement, drill  and  reserves,  they  have  unrealistic  armies  with  ultra  powerful beast that trance anything that moves, with 20 year old game mechanics repackaged every 5 years and sold at a phenomanal price that spotty teenagers are all to pleased to pay. Crap game, slick marketing. The list goes ever on.

We take the works of Tolkien and Moorcock to be actual historical accounts. In our careful reading we found that fantasy battle is an extension of the mechanics of historical battle. In Undying Lands you get all the tactical subtleties of historical wargaming plus an array of  fantastic beasts, sorcery, special attributes and their associated benefits and problems. Whether your intent is fair or foul, regardless of your Balrog  bodyguard, the ‘ordinary’ rank and file is the tactically most  important  soldier  on  your  battlefield  and  that  his  physical constraints is on par with his historical counterpart.

What’s  new  in Undying Lands? Basing is much more integrated and flexible, The command control system has been streamlined and clarified, troop and weapon characteristics have been amalgamated into a single versatile and variable factor. The result is a streamlined, integrated and smooth game that plays faster.

First, we define the combat and manoeuvre characteristics of your troops, then deal with command, control and orders starting from the top  down.  Next we generate the battle  field,  then  the  sections  on the game turn, movement, combat, missile fire, results and victory conditions.  Finally,  there's  a  section on 'how to  win and common battlefield, tactical and organisational errors. HAVE FUN.

—description from the rulebook

description:
Орлеан: Собор ("Orléans: Cathedral") is a level II promo Place Tile for Orléans.
Having this tile adds 1 to your development status for all purposes.

It was created specifically for the Russian edition of the base game. The prototype was The Cathedral of the Dormition (Uspenskiy sobor) in the Moscow Kremlin.

This was included as a part of Orléans: Promo Ortskarten N°2.

Free Print and Play version.

The Space Shuttle PK100A crashed somewhere in Texas on the unmanned maiden flight (Project Icarus).
Your team will search the crash site for items scattered around. Move items to the Investigation hangar for further investigation.
Could the cause be a broken bolt in control system, located in tail? Or an electric fire in a wing? Find out... Score Prestige Points during investigation. It is kind of cooperative -   but the most effective team will win.

Main elements are the crash site board with items (cubes) scattered around. Items are moved to the Investigation hangar board

Assistant (meeples) activates items by card play.

Deck building.
Primary mechanism is deck build. It is essential to build your deck according to actual situation on boards. Situation change quickly during a game, with a final dramatic race. You must discard useless cards. Correct Evidence cards must be discarded to your Personal file Board for end game scoring.

—description from the designer

You are the CEO of a drinking water company in a big country. The "I-Sports Festival" has been announced within this country, and a new stadium-building project has been established. You and other CEOs have signed a contract to contribute financially to the building the stadium and to put your company logos on it.

The CEOs have started a campaign to sell their products at a fair price in the developing market and to raise the profile of the company through a variety of corporate activities, aiming to become one of the most well-known companies in the country after the completion of the stadium.

BEVERAGE combines a worker-placement mechanism and a bidding system, with the game being partially inspired by Age of Steam and 18xx. The players are CEOs of various beverage companies. A new sports stadium is being built, and each CEO wants to get the stadium built and land the sponsorship rights. The progress of construction is the game clock.

Players decide on the price of their drinking water, which must be produced and shipped, taking into account market trends and other factors. Products will be judged by the market and bought based on their price! Each player then receives the revenue generated.

Starting with the first player who sold products, the company will use the funds earned to operate their company, including corporate activities to help to raise awareness of their company. However, the amount of tax to be paid by raising the level of awareness will gradually increase, and of course, it will be necessary to place the worker in charge of the chosen activity in the respective area in order to take the action.

In addition, the players who issued stock during the course of corporate activity gain additional capital and the merits of additional recognition at the time when dividends are paid. However, if dividends are not paid, the player will have a disadvantage that can greatly reduce the reputation.

Upon completion of the stadium, the companies' name recognition will be increased according to the amount of money invested in the stadium. The CEO of the most well-known company thanks to all these activities wins.

『蒸気の時代』や『１８XX』のシステムを一部参考にして制作した、商品を販売し収入を得て、収入をもってアクションを実施し、勝利点の取得と維持をしながら、ゲーム終了時最も勝利点を得ていたプレイヤーが勝利する「ワーカープレイスメント+ビッディングシステムを合わせた」ゲームです。

description:
CLASH ! is a Wargame system with figurines that acts as a bridge between players of level, experience and even fro different periods.

The player embodies his entire army and makes decisions for each officer. It does not emphasize combat formations and is easy to learn.

CLASH! is not intended to be used in competition as it leaves a bit of improvisation to the players. In several places, the rule proposes solutions only.

The game is played with figurines, grouped in constituted units. Each figurine represents a certain number of real men, therefore a unit can represent from a troop to a regiment or more, generally a company, a squadron or equivalent.

Army lists are available online

—description (extract) from the ruleset

English rules in progress / French rules v1.0

Army Lists for Two Hundred Years 5mm Wargames Rules 1700-1900. Including lists for the Marlburian Period, Great North War, 18th Century, Seven Years War, American War of Independence, Revolutionary Wars, Napoleonic Wars, India 1840-1850, Mexico, Crimean War, Mid 19th Century, American Civil War, Mexican War, Late 19th Century and British Colonial Wars.

These army lists are unusual in that they contain between 80 and 100 armies from the period 1700 – 1900. Virtually every army list is taken from a specific battle or campaign and is given at least a paragraph of background information – so, unlike other books of army lists, it is much more a compilation of historical research adapted to a specific set of rules. The contents page lists:

Marlburian period (5 Armies)
Great Northern War (2 Armies)
Seven Years War (6 Armies)
American War of Independence (3 Armies)
French Revolutionary Wars (4 Armies)
Napoleonic Wars (26 Armies)
Indian Campaign 1840-1850 (2 Armies)
Mexican American Wars (2 Armies)
Crimean (3 Armies)
Mid-19th Century (3 Armies including Garibaldi – Italian War of Independence)
ACW and Mexican Wars (9 Armies)
Late 19th Century (7 Armies including USA and Indian Wars)
British Colonial 1879-1890 (6 Armies including Zulu, Afghan & Mahdist)

The last two pages, 65 and 66, contain rule amendments and corrections.

description:
Cogbug is a mechanical gearboard tabletop game created in Estonia.
You have to PLACE cogwheels, SPIN gear systems and MOVE your bot minions the fastest across the field to your opponent’s base in order to win! 
But beware: the road to victory is not easy: the trails may not be straight and your opponents will do anything to stop you!

Gucio - black kitty - is missing. His trail leads us to the meadow. No wonder that the curious and mischievous kitty runs here among the grass, since there are so many wonderful butterflies around. There are so many of them that it seems impossible to count them all. Who of you will be smarter and more perceptive? Find out!

Kotek i motylki is a game that supports counting in a fun and emotional way. In addition, players must demonstrate perceptiveness and reflexes to indicate flower tokens with the correct result as soon as possible.

Purpose of the game: Players count the butterflies appearing on faceup Butterfly cards, and then seal the result token. With the correct calculations, they can get a card. The player with the most Butterfly cards at the end of the game wins.

—description from the publisher

Do you know Viking Tappi? He has a long, red beard, likes to drink hot chocolate and lives in the Whispering Forest. On the occasion of his birthday, he organized a party for his friends with reindeer Chichotek and the bird Drobniaszek. Roll the dice and complete randomly drawn tasks on your way to Tappi's party.
Remember: it's all about fun here!

Purpose of the game: Get to Tappi's party as soon as possible!

This game is for 2-5 people and can be played by the whole family and company friends. The main thing before the game is delicious to eat, as it causes appetite.
Your goal is to get the most points, and for that you will need to collect from spices aromatic seasonings - mixes, prepare delicious dishes, and also discover countries where these spices grow.

Northern Italian, set collection, card game similar to Scopa, but played with a special italian suits deck of 52 cards. Better with 4 players for a joint scoring tactics. The king card is wild, the ace can "clean" the cards on the table but not scoring a scopa, the deuce of swords is worth 1 point (Dobelon), also a Napoli sequence basically a set of ace, two and three or more of the same suit that could give a extra scoring points.

The pair of players reaching 120 points is declared winner.

Players play the role of characters who are handling money and shopping. There are stores with products and paths leading around them on the board placed at the center of the table. Each player starts the game with three coins.

The game has unique movement mechanic - player rolls a die with icons, move their figurine along any possible path, and stop at the first space with the same icon. They collect as many coins as they passed along the way. Then they can reveal and buy products adjacent to their position. If the die lands on a shopping cart, they can buy any of the opened products or roll again.

Because the products have different prices, the players have to balance getting and spending their coins.

The game ends when a player has five products in their shopping cart. Scoring - if a product has the icon of the player’s character or a star, it is worth two points, otherwise it is only worth one point. The player with the most points wins.

The game has an advanced mode included, in which players can close certain stores to block other players.

—description from the publisher

description:
Maternity & Dental Clinics is a new mini expansion for Rush M.D. that introduces two new modules which expand the game's Outpatient clinic.

In Maternity Clinic, you need to examine pregnant women to determine the gender of their baby(ies). In some cases the mother will have to be admitted in a Ward right away and have a surgery to give birth right away.

In order to identify each baby's gender or to determine if the pregnant ladies need to go into labor, you will need to perform an Ultrasound, in the same fashion that you perform this in the Ultrasound section of the new Examinations Board, by using the special see-through red plastic chip to locate one or more gender symbols on the back of the top Maternity Diagnosis card. For each symbol you locate, you must place the corresponding tile(s) on the mother's card. If instead of finding gender symbols during the ultrasound, you locate a scalpel symbol, this means that the mother-to-be needs to give birth immediately to ensure the health of her baby. You will need to Hospitalize the mother to a Ward bed and take her to an Operating Room soon! In the Operating Room you will need to bring a Doctor and a Nurse to deliver the baby!

In the Dental Clinic, you will get to admit Patients from the Outpatient Clinic who need to have their teeth checked up and cleaned. In order to perform a teeth cleanup, you will take the cubes and throw them like dice close by to the Dental Clinic board. Each cube(tooth) has one side that is colored black. Using your tweezers, you will need to place each tooth on the available slots of the Mouth tile but making sure that the black side of each tooth is facing down.

description:
In Hex Code, players take the role of computer hackers competing to break into each others' computer the quickest. Each turn players hack their way into their target's firewall by placing shapes dependent on the roll of dice. Once the firewall grid has started to fill, players can start guessing their target's password. If the player's six-letter guess contains any characters in the correct position, the target must say so; however, if a player's guess shares similarities to their target's security layer or firewall code, they trigger a trap. The target can immediately connect a node on their microchip, eventually allowing them to discern individual letters in their target's password. Overload firewalls, rewire microchips, and catch people in security traps to guess your target's password and prove you're the fastest hacker around!

Hex Code is a free-to-play printable game designed during the COVID-19 crisis, and will remain available here and through the publisher's website forever, with continued support.

—description from the designer

description:
January 1919. Poland returned to the map of Europe only two months earlier. Now the Republic desperately needs reforms which can be carried out only by Sejm. Even though the country doesn't even have its borders set up, the Chief of State calls for democratic election which will allow to choose the Representatives of the whole society. It's time for the first election campaign of the Second Polish Republic!

"Wybory 1919" is a dynamic boardgame in which the players are the leaders of four main political forces in the interwar Poland. They try to lead their parties to victory in the upcoming elections. By playing action cards, they compete for support in four main social groups: peasantry, city dwellers, workers and the minorities. They can increase their own popularity and place a supporter meeple in their color in one of the groups, decrease others' support, manipulate the regional elections' order or even cancel elections in some constituencies by calling in the military actions in the region. Each player gets an identical deck of 9 action cards at the start of the game, but draws up to the limit of three cards at random in their turn - reshuffling the discard pile if the draw pile runs out. Players need to strategize based on their current hand and pay attention to the cards that their opponents have already used.

Whoever gets the majority in each of the three groups favoured in the current round's constituency, wins the seat for their representative in that round of elections. The party that gets more representatives than any of the others after the last round is resolved, wins the game and assists in writing the Second Polish Republic's history!

The game was commissioned by the Social Communication Bureau at Chancellery of the Sejm of the Republic of Poland for the 100th anniversary of the first elections in Second Polish Republic. It's meant as an educational tool in teaching history and promotional item for the Sejm. As such, it hasn't been available in retail. The instructions booklet contains more detailed historical background on the elections of 1919-1920 in Poland. The action cards provide some basic information on the leaders of each political side as well as flavor texts in the form of newspaper quotes from that time.
publishers: Biuro Komunikacji Społecznej Kancelarii Sejmu RP

description:
Since the 1990s, Tokyo’s old Electric Town has evolved into the beating heart of pop culture. A dazzling mix of neon lights, anime, cafés, and arcades. 
In Akihabara, players compete to design the most iconic buildings along Chūo Dōri, the district’s famous main street.

Each player manages four buildings where they will open themed shops: Cafes, karaoke bars, restaurants, purikura photo booths, and arcades.

On your turn, you’ll draw biz cards to reveal new shops. You can keep drawing to find better combinations, but if the total of gashapon machines exceeds three, an inspection shuts you down for the turn! Knowing when to stop and when to risk it is key.

Once you’ve chosen your shops, you place them in your buildings, floor by floor. Aligning shops of the same brand vertically creates taller and more valuable businesses, while achieving color majorities across the same floor gives horizontal points. Completing an entire floor across all your buildings earns you a gashapon sign, which marks special floors that can score extra points later.

When someone completes two full buildings, the final round begins. Players then reveal their neon billboard, which doubles the score of one chosen building.

In the end, the winner is the player whose skyline shines the brightest, with the best mix of tall businesses, colorful floors, and perfectly placed gashapons!

"Asking for a Friend: The party game that might get weird" is a party game where all players are both asking and answering a question each round. The official goal is to collect the most points over ten rounds of play. The real goal is to finally have an excuse to ask your friend's the questions you've never asked before.

At the start of a round, a question card is drawn that has a blank. (e.g. "How often do you _____?"). Each player writes a prompt on a piece of paper that fills in the blank to make a complete question (e.g. "wake up before your alarm", "cry", or "fart and blame it on someone else"). All the prompts get shuffled, and each player draws a prompt. Each player answers the prompt they drew (which is sometimes the one they wrote). After everyone has answered, a voting card is revealed and the players vote for the winning answer based on the criteria on the voting card. (One round the voting might be based on which answer is "Most likely to disappoint your parents" and the next round might be based on who gave the "Most wholesome answer"). The winning answer gets the voting card, which is worth a point. However, great answers are made in response to great questions. The author of the prompt that spurred the winning answer will receive a reward card. Then a new round begins.

If you don't want to answer a question, you can take a penalty card, but you might have to answer more questions later. Reward cards can do anything from telling someone else to get you a drink to letting you pick who answers which questions. Use your reward cards wisely, but don't forget to use them.

The game is concluded after 10 Question Cards/rounds have been completed (or really, however many you want to play!) The player with the most victory points is the winner.

The cards are minimalist and each deck has it's own color (white, black, and orange). There is nothing inherently dirty about this game, but you can make it very adult/mature if you'd like. You also could play with kids and grandparents at a family reunion and ask family friendly questions. The maturity level of the game depends on the prompts that are written.

description:
Camelot is in chaos, since King Arthur's throne is now up for the taking. Use your wits and arsenal to defeat your foes.

To begin the riot, you attack the defender with a card(s) from your hand. Other players can always join the attack by playing any matching valued cards. The defender can flee, pass, or attempt to be victorious by defeating all the cards. If your friend is about to triumph, you can play another card at the last minute. Will it be enough to push him over the edge?

Using an arsenal card or a jester card can greatly effect the course of each round. Successfully defeat cards to earn more arsenal cards. Throughout the game, you can also earn crowns and scepters to help you in your quest to become the new ruler of Camelot. Three crowns wins you the game! Do you have the strength to be victorious? Join the riot and we shall see!

Each card represents innovative inventions and discoveries.
Players lay these cards to develop their own civilization.
You need to lay cards clever or you will fall behind in the flow of innovations!
You win if you have most victory points at the end of a game.

You can also play solo.
In solo, you play against Napoleon, a mighty imaginary player.

This is a free PnP expansion for Welcome to..., available to all in these troubled times.

The expansion contains a sheet with a new twist, 3 City Plan cards and both multiplayer and solo rules (Classic & New Solo Mode).

-description from designer

Mars Rover is a solitaire game of Mars exploration. You control a robotic Mars rover across the surface of Mars. Your mission is to collect soil samples and upload the analysed data. You have a three-day deadline and need to contend with the harsh Martian environment, solar flares and marsquakes.

The game is card-driven by a single deck of cards with the status of your rover updated on a game playmat. During the game you will be regularly draw a card from the explore deck and a single element on the card will determine an outcome or provide event relevant information. Each game has a soil data upload target. During your exploration of the Martian terrain you will have to balance energy use for exploration, drilling for soil samples, uploading data and navigating away from rockslides.

Your mission is limited to three days and when you decide to end the day you can repair and recharge your rover. There are, however, opportunities for energy recharging during the day and a push-your-luck mechanism when considering whether to explore further or stop for the day.

The game ends on day 3 and winning is determined on whether you have met your soil data upload target and made it back to base. If your rover ever has 0% energy across drill, gyroscope and battery power, your rover is powerless on Mars and your mission has failed.

—description from the publisher
