description:
2 groups are fighting for the domination of Color City. Color City is represented by a grid in the center of the game board. The youngest player starts the game and the players take turns playing until the end of the game.

Each turn is divided in 5 steps:

1) Explore

     The player pitches one of the cubes of his HQ, with one of these fingers, towards the city.


2) Power activation (optional action)

     Once during each game, each team can decide to use the power of their gang.
     To do this, the team checks its power box and applies its effect.
     Once checked, a power box can no longer be checked during the rest of the game.
     Power of the green gang - Lucky Day: allows you to take back the cube pitched this turn, replace it on its HQ and p it again.
     Purple gang power - Purple Pain: allows you to destroy one of the cubes located in an opposing HQ.


3) Fight

     If the cube stops outside the city, it is destroyed.
     We consider that a cube stops on an area as soon as it covers 1 mm of its surface.
     A destroyed cube is placed in the game box and can no longer be used for the rest of the game.


4) Conquer

     If the cube has not been destroyed, color the area on which it stopped with your marker and then take the cube back.
     When you take one of your cubes back, put it on your HQ.
     The city is made up of 81 squares. If a cube covers several boxes, color all of these boxes.


5) Victory check and End of turn.

     If no player has won or lost the game, his opponent plays.


End conditions:

A) If a player succeeds in forming a horizontal or vertical line of 9 spaces with his color, he wins the game.

     Diagonal lines do not count.



B) If a player succeeds in forming a square of 16 boxes (4 x 4 boxes) with his color, he wins the game.

C) If a player succeeds in eliminating his opponent's 4 cubes, he wins the game.

     Reminder: cubes are destroyed when they stop in an area they must not occupy.


—description from the designer

Solo game, based on Race to the Rhine mechanics, simulating the liberation of Brittany during August and September '44.
The 8th US Corps of Troy Middleton has the mission to liberate the region and secure the Breton ports for use them as supply bases.
The player commands the 4 divisions of the 8th US Corps and his task is, in a limited time to score a maximum of Medals, by destroying German units, occupying strategic cities, besieging and capturing the ports and preventing their destruction by the Wehrmacht.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups

description:
Ralph got out of the game machine and now walks on the Internet! Is there enough space on the Web for such a big guy? Check the strength of the World Wide Web servers along with the characters in the Disney animated film "Ralph Breaks the Internet". The one who overloads the most servers will be the winner.

In the deck of cards are four characters + a joker (replaces any character) with numbers from 1 to 16. At the beginning, 12 cards are dealt to each. In each round, you must select one card face up. Then all the cards are laid out in descending order on the table. If your card is the fourth in a row, column or pile - you take it. These are your victory points. After 12 rounds, the player with the most points is declared the winner.

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Imperial Bayonets: We Were Not Cowards – Sedan 1870
description:
"Imperial Bayonets: We Were Not Cowards - Sedan 1870" is an operational wargame simulation of the last battle of the Second Empire. It uses a rules-engine heavily inspired by The Library of Napoleonic Battles rules system.

Several scenarios allow for a quicker French response to Prussian maneuvers the day before creating a salvageable situation for the French. Each hex is approximately 1KM and units are divisions which can break down into their subordinate brigades. Maintaining a chain of command allows players to freely act with their units, while units outside of command range must rely on initiative. Special rules are included for French Chassepot and Mitrailleuse and the obliterating power of Prussian Krupp artillery.
language_dependency: 3: Moderate in-game text - needs crib sheet or paste ups
family: Players: Wargames with Rules Supporting Only Two Players

description:
Bacterium is a free PNP territory-control game for two Players. You each represent different strains of bacteria, bred in a lab, which are competing to spread widely into as many Petri dishes as possible. But beware of the Penicillin mold floating in the air...

To play this game you will need:
This rule set
A 6x6 grid map
A sheet of game counters, but you can substitute poker chips, coins, buttons, beads, marbles, or any other game pieces of two different colors as Player pieces, plus 6 additional pieces of another color as Penicillin markers
1d6

Each Player starts by placing one game piece anywhere on the board. Players then place new game pieces on the board. They may choose to do this in one of two ways: they may place a single game piece anywhere on the board. Or they may make a “trail” by following any straight-line path from one of their already-placed game pieces (as a Bacterium, after all, you want to spread yourself as far as you can.) The Player then "reproduces" by placing his pieces in each empty spot in between along the three-space trail, reflecting the rapid growth of the bacteria. Every Petri dish game space he has thereby infected then becomes a potential source for further infections.

In addition to moving, game pieces can be captured by surrounding a Bacterium and starving it of resources so it dies and is replaced by the Player's Bacteria.

In addition, there are Penicillin Spores that are floating around in the lab which will land randomly, killing the Bacteria in some Petri dishes and replacing them. Every so often, Players roll dice to determine where the Penicillin has landed. If there are any Bacteria game pieces in these marked spaces, they are removed and replaced with an Opponent’s game piece.

Play continues until neither Player is able to make a legal move. The Player whose Bacteria occupy the most Petri dishes at the end of the game, is the winner.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Dr. Biscuits' Radical Road Trip: 60 Fun Games to Play in Your Car
description:
60 Fantastic Games To Play in Your Car!


     Survive a License Plate Asteroid Field
     Play a new version of H-O-R-S-E using the radio
     Spot themed bumper stickers for Points
     Spark a dance contest at the next rest stop
     Send a postcard to the White House (included!)
     Trick your friends into meowing like a cat
     Play games like Duck Duck Bruce, Flip Dingus, and Ronjon Chico Bonbon … And that’s just scratching the surface!


Dr. Biscuits' Radical Road Trip is the brainchild of Barry and Jason, the creators of Game Night in a Can. Just as they did with their first successful game, Barry and Jason used their comedic skills and fun-loving competitive spirit to create 60 brand new games to play in the car. There are 6 categories of games to choose from, each one containing 10 games. The Categories are Everlasting Games, Logical Games, Ridiculous Games, Games for Humanity, Creative Games and Games Outside the Car. It's been called "Cranium for the Car" by some and "the most fun I've had in a car since you were conceived" by others

—description from the publisher

Grow Your Village... with this expansion to D6.
Now you can have three more classes to choose from. DO you need a card? Draw it from one of the three handy-dandy card carts. Did you slay a monster? Collect your two coins from the Village coin tray. Want to raise your stats? Now you can drink from the actual Fountain of You.
A glorious hunter deserves a glorious Village!

—description from the publisher

With Dragondado children and adults have fun while working on their concentration, reflex-speed, emotional self control, fine motor skills and math abilities.

A compelling board game in which participants will have to compete against each other to complete 14 mathematical mental calculation tournaments with speed and dexterity in the shortest time possible!

After training in each of the 14 challenges, players can challenge each other in the Knights Arena to find out who the Dragondado Knight will be.

Every year in the Kingdom of Ludus there is a festival dedicated to fairy tales. But this year, during the night, some strange elements entered the stories, completely changing their meaning. Can you find these items? To do this, players will have to rely on their sense of touch, find the intruder and put the fairy tales in the correct order.

Tactoo encourages tactile, manual and attention skills.

Pick a side of the game board - colors or animals - and then pass out the tiles to each player. Ready? Set? MATCH!

It's a race to match all of your tiles with the colors or animals on the game board. But here's the catch - Each tile only matches with one specific space on the game board.

The smuggler has to go from his Island hideout to the four harbors and back again to win the game. The four police boats try to capture the smuggler. Movement is determined by a die. A change of direction while moving is allowed. If the smuggler lands on a police boat, it has to return to one of the harbors for repair. If a police boat lands on the smuggler, the game ends and the police player wins.

—user summary

Paintbrush is a deeply strategic game coming soon the Blinks Game System. Paintbrush, like all great strategy games, uses simple mechanics to create complex situations, all made possible by Blinks, the world's first smart tabletop game system. AI-powered game pieces act as both brush and canvas, and players take turns rearranging the dynamic pieces to spread their color onto as many blank canvasses as they can. Simple mechanics mean the game can be picked up easily, but the strategic depth allows for repeated play and the possibility of mastery.

Paintbrush is a game for 2 to 6 players, played with 6 or more Blinks. A typical game of Paintbrush can take anywhere from 15 to 30 minutes, with larger player counts and more Blinks often leading to longer games. Each player selects a unique Paintbrush color, and take turns moving individual Blinks around the board to spread their color onto blank canvasses. A canvas which has been painted entirely with one color becomes a Paintbrush itself. Once all the Blinks have been converted to Paintbrushes, the game is over, and whichever player has the most Paintbrushes wins.

—description from the publisher

Raid, the newest game on the Blinks Game System, places you in command of a viking horde, competing with other players to grab as much territory as you can. It's an intense game of strategy and prediction, as each player secretly inputs orders into the AI-powered Blinks game pieces, then watches as skirmishes play out in real time. It's the kind of unique analog/digital hybrid experience that's only possible on Blinks, the world's first smart tabletop game system. The game started as a post on the Move38 developer forum in early February, and has since been iterated on and perfected with the help of the Move38 team.

In Raid, 2 to 4 players compete to conquer an island made of Blinks. The game requires at least 6 Blinks, and takes 10-20 minutes, depending on the size of the island and number of players. Each round, players close their eyes, then take turns opening their eyes and making an attack on the island with their longboat. Once all players have attack, all eyes are opened and the game calculates the outcome of the round. Armies spread from the coast into the center of the island, competing for a dwindling set of unoccupied territories. When all coastal territories have been attacked and no more longships can land, the game calculates which player conquered the largest territory, making them the winner.

—description from the publisher

Can't we all just get along?! It's your job to get these Blinks what they need most: extroverts want neighbors, introverts need their space.  It's a focused frenzy for one or many!

The Pieces display their preferred state and players have a short time window to separate pieces. Each round is different and the modular board allows before wild configurations.

This game really shines as a cooperative challenge to sort pieces in time leaving no piece behind.

in the mood is the second game from Stefan Breuer (head of youth welfare in Dorsten/Germany) which is offered for free download to the people during corona-pandemic. 
The city of Dorsten wants to bring some fun for youth and families. The game can be played pnp and in videomeetings and so on.

in the mood is a partygame about music and emotions. What do you think about "Highway to hell" during a candle-light-dinner? Fits perfect, is okay oder never ever?!?
Can the other players imagine what you do feel?

—description from the designer

in the mood ist das zweite Spiel von Stefan Breuer (Jugendamtsleiter Dorsten), das umsonst zum Download den Menschen während der Corona-Pandemie geschenkt wird. Die Stad Dorsten will den Jugendlichen und Familien etwas Spaß vermitteln. Das Spiel kann als pnp zu Hause gespielt werden, aber auch in Videomeetings.
in the mood ist ein Partyspiel über Musik und Emotionen. Was denkst du über "Highway to hell" beim Candlelight-Dinner? Passt perfekt, ist okay oder geht gar nicht?!?
Werden die anderen Spieler erraten können, was du fühlst?

—description from the designer (German)

The Battle of France was the first great tank battle, and perhaps the first major battle, of World War II. Of course, it was not called that at the time. People called it ‘Hitler’s War’ or ‘The Phony War’. Perhaps it was Hitler’s war, but it was far from phony. It was, in fact, the complete opposite and the Wehrmacht was out to prove it. France was to be the proving grounds that Germany’s Panzer tank divisions were to become feared on.

No one suspected a strategic pincer attack from the Ardennes forest, but rather through the Maginot Line. Nearly everyone believed that the Maginot Line would be the ultimate wall of defense against Germany. Heinz Guderian was the exception. His ground breaking idea of mechanized warfare created World War II. Here is where he would show the world the strength of a concentrated attack force and how an entire nation could be brought to its knees by a swift, decisive strike.

In Battle of France 1940 you play the role of either Guiderian or Charles De Gaulle. You make the decisions; Will you attack Dunkerk and cut off the British? Can you defend Paris against the ever growing German armies? Will your men continue to fight despite massive casualties and declining morale? How will you deal with supply shortages?

Combat cards simulate fog of war and new challenges to face your troops every round. Unique rules mimic the Blitzkrieg system of warfare and special combat features include divisional singularity, tank superiority, air support and retreat rules. Also featuring special territories showcasing the historic elements of the Ardennes Forest and the Maginot Line.

Battle of France 1940 is filled with factors beyond your control. However, the heart of the game is in how you position your forces so that the industry, morale and equipment of your opponent is destroyed, captured or commandeered.

Can you shape the fate of the world in a few short hours?

—description from the designer

The floating tribes of Typatia have lived under the tyrannical rule of The Empress for far too long. She has starved the people, abused the dinosaurs, and grown fat off the riches of the land.

As a Chief of Typatia, it is your duty to throw off the yoke of oppression and claim your tribe's rightful place as the leaders of Typatia.

Will you do this through economic prowess, scientific advancement, political negotiation, or pure military might? The choice is yours as you spread your influence across the floating lands.

Defiant is a 4X bag-building game set in a land of floating islands and battling dinosaurs.

Each turn you will use the cubes you drew (at the end of the previous turn) to activate actions. Each action requires 2 cubes and will increase in power if you are willing to spend your rare bronze, silver, or even gold cube to complete the action. Be sure to plan ahead or your previously placed cubes could end up wasted.

Air battles occur when 2 sets of flyers encounter each other. Transported raptors & rexes add nothing to these battles. Only the number & strength of each army's flyers matter. If the attacking flyers win, they may then drop their land units onto the island below.

These dropped units will then instigate a land battle if defenders are present. These battles are more tricky, with secret modifier cards influencing each side's strength.

A player may be strong in the air, but weak on land, unable to take over defended islands. Or a player may be strong on land, but weak in the air, unable to transport their raptors and rexes to new islands.

The game ends when a player has completed enough objectives. These include:
•Completing 1 of the 4 upgrade tracks.
•Winning an air battle.
•Winning a land battle.
•Defeating the Empress.
•Completing the special objective which is drawn at the beginning of the game.

Players then count up the points from their objectives, controlled hexes, and gold/silver/bronze cubes.

Defiant comes from our love of bag-building and 4X games. We aimed to build a 4X game that could be played quicker than a normal 4X game, yet still have the same scope and feel. The bag building element helps in this endeavor, as players can be planning their turns from 1 turn to the next (since they've already drawn the next turn's cubes).

Note: Hardcore 4X gamers can choose to play to a higher objective thresh-hold for an even more sprawling and vast game of Defiant.

-description from designer

boardgame: Who's Got the Baby? Card Game
description:
Who’s Got the Baby is a card game with a fun twist on the Mardi Gras King Cake baby tradition!

Mardi Gras season means lots of King Cakes and trying to find the Baby inside! Although the Baby symbolizes luck to whoever finds it in their slice of king cake, the downside for them is that they are responsible for purchasing the next king cake and throwing the next Mardi Gras party! That downside is why you don’t want to be the one stuck with the baby in this game! The last player left in the game and not eliminated by the King Cake Baby card wins! Your turn consists of two steps: playing as many Action cards as you want and then drawing a card from the Draw pile to end your turn. After your turn ends, the next person can go by playing Action cards first and then drawing a card to end their turn. Playing Action Cards in the game such as Fortune Teller, Bead Throw Attack, and the Crawfish Block help you avoid the Baby in the Draw pile at all costs! That’s it! Last person standing wins the game! The key is to avoid those Baby Game Over cards! Who’s got the baby? Hopefully, not you!

Includes: 54 cards, box, and instructions.

The horrors of trench warfare rage across Europe in 1916. Machine guns are cutting brave soldiers to ribbons when they go "over the top" and are confronted with mazes of wire. Tanks are appearing more frequently on the battlefield, as well as biplanes which seem to evolve every day. The major source of havoc, however, is artillery, which rains hell from above on a regular basis. Not to mention the poison gas.

1916: The Card Game is a tense filler for two players, and includes all of the above. The rules are simple, but the choices aren't. Players represent the opposing commanders of a small stretch of trench line. They draw and play cards from a common deck, but some can only be used by one side. Some cards can be played alone or in combination, and what to play and what to hold in hand can be critical decisions. Some cards have multiple uses. Biplanes, for example, can be used for recon, assault support, or aerial intercept. Victory can be total, major, minor or, rarely, the result can be a stalemate.

-description from designer

The Ragnarok expansion adds a set of viking-themed sellswords to the game. Among them are a number of "norse god" cards that feature the new "passive" keyword and add their own unique rules and twists to the battlefield whenever they're in play.

In Crimson Company, two opposing players take on the roles of adventurous noblemen in a cut-throat fantasy world. In a battle of wits, they assemble armies of sellswords, from hardened warriors and shady merchants to mythical creatures as old as time.

Your goal in a match of Crimson Company is to conquer two of the three castles in the center. You conquer a castle by amassing more strength than your opponent in the castle's lane by the time that lane is scored. A lane is scored once a player owns at least four cards in it.

Cards represent a large variety of characters, all of which have specific strength values and unique effects. The latter are either one-time instant effects that happen when a card is played, or they are triggered in specific phases of the game. Four randomly drawn cards from the character deck are "on offer". You may bid coins on only one of those at a time. Then your opponent decides to either pay you off, doubling the coins you bid, or pass and let you play the card.

-description from designer

The Harpoon Player's Handbook is a compilation of all the charts and tables contained in the rulebook. Use of this playing aid greatly assists the flow of play in multiple player games, as all of the data is right at hand, along with references to the appropriate section of the rulebook.

“Before you looms the dimly lit entrance of the most feared dungeon in the land. It is called the GAMOT. Within the Gamot lie dangers untold, for no hero has yet returned from its deep depths. The journey here has not been an easy one. You dismount from your trusty steed and ready your sword and shield. If you conquer the Gamot, you will earn the greatest glory in the land and with it a place among the King’s personal guard. A worthy position for a hero such as yourself!

As you approach the entrance to the Gamot, you can smell the foul stench of death and are overcome with an unexplained dread. While you steel your nerves against this dark air, you hear the sound of hoof beats behind you. Your horse has fled in fear… You are truly alone now. You step into the entrance of the Gamot, filled with determination. As you do so, however, the floor gives way and you find yourself suddenly tumbling down an incline. You hit the ground below with a hard thud. After taking a moment to regain your bearings, you search in the darkness for your sword and shield. They are nowhere to be found! Rising to your feet, you see that you have fallen into a long corridor lined with doors. A haunting laugh echoes in the dungeon. The only thing that stands between you and whatever horrors await you in the dungeon is your will to live and your desire to prove yourself the greatest hero in the land…

Will you survive the Gamot?”

Gamot is a solitaire dungeon crawling card game, designed to be quick and playable on-the-go. You take the role of an adventuring hero as you attempt to navigate the perils of the dungeon. You will search for weapons and items in which to defend yourself, while fighting fearsome monsters and drinking potions to keep your health up! No two games are ever the same!


     Arm yourself with swords, bows and warhammers, or locate powerful weapons such as the Golden Trident!
     Use special items like the Magic Ring to rearrange the shape of the dungeon!
     Choose wisely when to face a threat head on and when to run!
     Face monsters such as werewolves, black knights, great worms and the dreaded and deadly lich!
     Search the halls of the dungeons for potions to help you recover from your wounds suffered at the hands of these brutal beasts!



Gamot is the perfect game for on-the-go players! Gamot requires little space to play, plays as quickly as you can make decisions and can be carried in your pocket. Designed to be played while waiting in lines, travelling on buses or trains, or on your break at work, Gamot also works for filling in time while you wait for your turn at the gaming table. With only a deck of 54 standard playing cards (and some small counters that you can easily replace with your memory or a scrap of paper and a pencil), and developed to appeal to both your mind and your wallet, Gamot provides a great and inexpensive dungeon crawling experience that won't break the bank!

Gamot is easily learned, but tough to master. Gamot is also family-friendly, and requires only basic math skills to get started.

—description from the designer
language_dependency: 2: Some necessary text - easily memorized or small crib sheet

boardgame: Task Force: Scenario Book 5 – Battleships at Midway
description:
Battleships at Midway

It has all the set ups for the battle between the Americans and Japanese ships at Midway in 1942.

It comes complete with all the ships that fought in the battle, damage charts for each ship, and notes and rules modifications for the battle.

This Scenario Book uses the Rules Task Force or you can use another set of rules that you like.

It has all the set ups for the battle of Leyte between the Americans and Japanese in 1944.

This Scenario Book uses the Rules Task Force or you can use another set of rules that you like.

boardgame: Task Force: Scenario Book 7 – Battle of Bizerte 1940: The Franco-Italian War of 1940
description:
Battle of Bizerte 1940 - The Franco-Italian War of 1940

It has all the set ups for the battle between the Italians and French in 1940.

It comes complete with all the ships that fought in the battle, damage charts for each ship, and notes and rules modifications for the battle.

description:
Autumn Grove Manor is a co-op game for 2-6 players. A team of ghost hunters, armed with only Lord Latimer's Journal, must collect 6 gems which can be combined to create a 'weapon' to defeat Palmershu, the Demon God, who has claimed the manor as his own. You'll have to exorcise the ghosts, while avoiding holes in the ground and searching the manor for items and gems, but beware, Palmershu may appear at the end of any round, and he's not pleased that you're trying to exorcise him.

The game is set up by shuffling the room cards, placing them upside down on the board so each room can't be seen and placing all the players at the front door and all the ghosts and the Palmershu marker are placed in the cloth bag.
During Part 1 A player has 8 action points each turn, which can be used for moving, exorcising ghosts, using items, giving an item to an ally, or opening chests or doors. Once a player opens either a chest or a door, they've alerted the ghosts to their presence and their turn immediately ends and they turn over a card from the ghost deck.
During the end of turn players must add more ghosts onto the board using a pair of D20 dice pulling ghosts (or Palmershu) randomly from the bag.
The aim of Part 1 is to search the house for 6 gems, spread across the house in the different rooms.

In Part 2 the board is flipped and Palmershu starts his final attack as the team attempt to destroy him. 
All the items you've collected in part 1 are carried over and can be played any round before anyone rolls to attack. The number of gems you collected will decreases the number of cards revealed for where Palmershu will appear.

Part 2 is split into 5 sections:
Palmershu's Arrival
From the Palmershu Deck reveal the correct number of card (the more gems you found in Part 1, the less places he may attack from)
Character Actions
The Characters can then use items and move up to 3 spaces in any order.
Palmershu Appears
The top card of Palmershu's attack deck is revealed which reveals which card and which power is used.
Characters Attack
The Characters who are in range of Palmershu or ghosts then attack them.
Palmershu Attacks
Finally the ghosts and Palmershu will attack the Characters.

To win the game the team must defeat Palmershu. However if all the ghosts are removed from the bag then the team must flee the house and the game is lost, or if any player loses all their hearts then the game is lost.
There are 4 levels of difficulty, which vary the number of ghosts on the board to begin and the number that appear each turn.

—description from the designer

Biplane Barmy Refuelled is the long awaited second edition of Biplane Barmy, the game of World War I fighter combat.

It uses the same core "choose & move" system but is the first choose and move game based on a hexagonal map.

Players control one or more planes each and secretly choose maneuvers each turn, then move all planes simultaneously and resolve firing.

The game is fast and furious, typically playable in around 15 minutes, making it ideal to fill on round a roleplaying session or as a tournament game.

The game uses simple mechanics for fast play, but doesn't sacrifice detail with stats for a wide range of aircraft and rules to handle all manner of specifics, from bombing to garros wedges.

—description from the publisher

I'm Not Saying You're Stupid... is a trivia game where players try to guess the right answer, but also guess which player has the most wrong answer.

The game contains 110 trivia cards with 4 questions on each card. It also contains 9 I'm Not Saying You're Stupid... cards.

At the beginning of the game each player gets paper and a pen to write their answers on. Also one player is designated as the score keeper. Each player also gets one of the I'm Not Saying You're Stupid...  cards.

To start one player draws a trivia card and reads the first question. Each player writes what they think the answer is. All of the questions have a numeric answer, and each player should only write a numeric answer. After every player has written their answer, each player places their  I'm Not Saying You're Stupid...  cards in front of the player whose answer they think is furthest from the true answer. Then the answer is revealed and players score points. 5 points for the correct answer, 2 points for the closest answer, and 1 point for placing the  I'm Not Saying You're Stupid... in front of the player who had the answer furthest from the correct answer. After scoring the next question is read. When all the questions are read from one card, the next player draws the next card. The game continues until one player reaches a predetermined amount of points or a predetermined amount of time has passed.
language_dependency: 4: Extensive use of text - massive conversion needed to be playable

description:
What If, But Also is a lighthearted party game in which 4 or more players compete to spin stories out of chaos and pull creative pitches out of wacky prompts — all to win the judge’s favor.

Each round, players come up with the best possible or worst possible scenario, based on the cards in play and in their hands. Scenarios are inspired by (however loosely players want), and must reference, a phrase created by 5 cards: 2 Link Cards and 3 Story Cards. These cards change between rounds.

Each turn, one player takes on the role of judge and all other players take on the role of storytellers. The judge determines whether they want to hear the best or worst possible scenario and indicate by flipping the "What If…" card to its positive or negative side. They draw the top two cards of the Link deck and play them face up. They then play a Story card from their own hand to begin the scenario. Storytellers play Story cards from their hands to supply options for 2 of the words in the phrase. These 2 words are different for each storyteller, meaning they’ll base their scenarios off slightly different phrases.

At the end of the turn, roles shift. The player to the left of the previous judge becomes the new judge. Each turn consists of three phases: judge’s setup, storyteller’s scenarios, and judge’s choice. Play continues until every player has had a chance to be judge twice — or keep going as long as you want! The game can be ended after any judge’s choice phase.

—description from the publisher

Bluff the Bullet is a poker-like game, with extra elements of bluffing and special card abilities. Players are dealt two separate hands, a “Scoring Hand” that determines if they win or lose, and a “Playing Hand,” whose cards have abilities that they can use to help improve their Scoring Hands.

At the start of the game, players are dealt five-card Scoring Hands, and are competing to have the best hand at the end of each round. But there’s one problem: While they start the game knowing what their cards are, that can change. Players are also dealt Playing Hands. During the game they will use abilities on these Playing Hand cards to learn about and change their and others’ Scoring Hands, to try to give themselves the best Scoring Hand possible.

Scoring Hands are ranked similarly (but not exactly) like poker, because each card has one of six suits and number value (from 1 to 7). The number values are important for scoring. Each card also has an ability based on its suit. When you use an ability, state which card(s) its ability is targeting. All abilities (except for bullets) affect Scoring Hand cards.

Beginning with the starting player and proceeding in clockwise order, each player takes a turn playing one card from their Playing Hand. The round ends when no one has any cards left to play. On your turn, must play a card, unless you’ve played a bullet card between your last turn and this one. In that case, you may still play a Playing Hand card, but it is optional. Whoever has the highest Scoring Hand at the end of the game is the winner.

—description from the publisher

It comes complete with all the ships that fought in the battle,damage charts for each ship,and notes and rules modifications for the battle.

boardgame: Magic: The Gathering – Core Set 2020 Spellslinger Starter Kit
description:
The Spellslinger Starter Kit is a learn-to-play product aimed at new players, including instructional materials that guide two players through their first game. It was sold in local game stores and mass market stores.

Unlike its previous installment (which was released on the same day as Guilds of Ravnica, but had no obvious connection to that set), this Starter Kit is marketed as part of the Core Set 2020 release. They are now mono-color decks, and the two face cards are given the foil treatment.

Contents

2 60-card preconstructed decks, each containing 5 rares.[3]
2 Two quick-start guides
1 Rules and world-of-Magic booklet
2 “do no shuffle”/"on your turn" cards
2 Spindown life counters
1 MTG Arena code card

In this box, you'll find 500 little men drawn by a famous Russian artist Tatiana Zadorozhniaya and 5 fast reaction games for different ages. Can you find the right man before everyone else does? The fastest one will get a pie!

6 exciting game modes for you to try out
A whole lot of fun for kids ages 4 and up
500 unique cute little men

—description from the publisher

В этой коробке полтысячи нарисованных человечков и пять весёлых игр на скорость и внимательность для разных возрастов. Сможете ли вы отыскать нужного человечка раньше всех? Самые быстрые получат в награду пирожок!

Развивает внимание и скорость реакции.
Подходит для детей от 4 лет.
500 уникальных рисунков человечков.

—description from the publisher (Russian)

In Pandora Cat, a.k.a. パンドラの猫, you're racing to reach a points threshold before any other player can, but in each round you never know whether you want to score high or low until it's almost over.

Each round, each player is dealt four cards from the 24-card deck. Each of the eight types of cards has a value on it as well as a special ability. Each player chooses one card from their hand, then they reveal these cards in clockwise order from the starting player. Players then do this again two more times, leaving them with one card in hand.

Your score for the round is the sum of the value of the card in your hand and the value of the cards you played. Normally the player with the highest score earns 5 points, with the players in second and third earning 3 and 1 points respectively — but if a player has played two "Pandora Cat" cards, then the player with the lowest score earns 5 points! If two players have each played two "Pandora Cat" cards, then scoring is normal, whereas three players having done this reverses the score once again.

Rotate the start player position each round, and complete multiple rounds until a player has scored at least 12, 14, or 16 points in a three-player, four-player, or five- or six-player game.

Wieczna Waśń ("Eternal Feud") is a strategic board game from 2 to 4 people / 8 + / 60-120 depending on the map of the land, in which 4 feuded races fight for dominance in a randomly created land.

No one remembers how the feud began, who came to the field or who first chewed on the stone ... the fact is that this feud seems to have always happened. People against the green horde of orcs from the north ... until one day the conflict joined the fallen, a great army of the dead rose up and went to war with the living ... in this eternal feud.

—description from the designer

"The once profitable mines of Altair have become overrun with the most sinister of beasts. Miners, knights, and sell-swords have all fallen at the claws of these foul creatures. With the Kingdom of Yule already on the verge of bankruptcy, the King cannot afford to lose control of these mines. No amount of death can stop his desire to retain his power.Lucky for you, your reputation as a Bounty Hunter has earned you a chance to prove your worth. Capturing criminals has been easy, surely your unique skill set will transfer to killing beasts. The King is willing to empty the last of the kingdom's coffers for the heads of these creatures. Will you fall amongst the treasure and corpses of the cavern or slay your enemies to claim your due glory?"

Dungeon Bounty is a fast paced hand management game designed to be played with a single standard deck of playing cards. On each turn, players flip two cards from the deck (dungeon). If the cards are a 1-10 of clubs (weapons), diamonds (gemstones), or spades (spells), the player chooses one to add to their play area. Face cards (Jacks, Queens, and Kings) are the bounties, the creatures you are hunting, and when they pop up as one of the two drawn cards from the dungeon, you need to defeat them using your attack power from weapons and spells. The more bounties you collect, the higher your score.

If you are unable to defeat a monster, they do damage to you, reducing your strength, which is represented by the 1-10 of hearts. If you are at 1 strength and are damaged by an enemy, you lose. If you manage to stay alive, the game ends after two trips through the dungeon. After you've depleted the deck the first time, you shuffle the discard pile and play through the deck a second time. Compare your score to the scoring guide and see your destiny.

—description from the publisher

The Imperial Japanese Army started developing small gunboats for close support of amphibious landings in 1924 and by 1927 had the AB-Tei gunboat. By WWII these would be diesel-powered.  They would be transported to the landing zones on merchant ships. They were also used as patrol boats in rivers, harbors and coastal zones and to escort supply barges. In this role they encountered PT boats.

The ‘57 mm’ guns were actually the Japanese Type 97 47 mm anti-tank gun or a British 6 pdr.  Contains 4 gunboats and state cards.

—description from the publisher

Pirates & Lasers is a strategic struggle between ghost pirates and incompetent laser-wielding tourists. Keep the ship sailing as long as you can while lasers tear your ship asunder, managing resources and preventing damage to critical areas.

This turn-based strategy game challenges players to survive laser attacks and repair the boat hull each turn to prevent sinking.

Ship Blinks, which have health and the ability to transfer health to any neighboring Ship Blink, and Hazard Blinks, which fire lasers out in three directions, damaging any part of the Ship in its path. A turn has three stages - Rearrange, Repair, and Lasers! During Rearrange, Ship and Hazard Blinks are moved to prevent damage. During Repair, resources can be moved from one part of the Ship to another. During Lasers!, the Hazard Blinks fire off their lasers, dealing damage to any part of the Ship in its path. The game ends when any part of the Ship hits zero health. See how many turns you can survive in a given scenario!

-description from publisher

In Pizza Delivery, you are a pizza delivery courier, and you must try to make the most profit out of one delivery day, both by delivering pizzas and by fulfilling your secret objectives. You can also use money to buy extra equipment if you think that will help you in the long run.

Whoever has the most money at the end of the game wins.

Each player moves his car by roll of a die on a map of the Weserbergland, a region in Northern Germany. Each player starts at Hannover and draws 3 destination cards. When a player arrives at one of his destinations, he must stop, discards his card and draws an event card. The player who first stopped at all 3 destinations and returned to Hannover wins the game.
This game is part of a serie concerning several regions.

—user summary

KAOS is a battle game where two sides of twenty fighters will have to defeat each other in order for the wish of their God Etharyl to be fulfilled. In KAOS each player represents an Etharyl, who will declare a wish before battle.

The player (an Etharyl God), before combat, can make sacrifices in his own team to resurrect fighters or make them more powerful, managing his team in order to avoid running out fighters. Also, the player can erase skills from rival fighters by exhausting glint points, in a limited way. During combat between fighters, destiny will decide.

The game is finished when an Etharyl runs out of fighters. At this moment, it must accept the wish of the winner.

—description from the designer

Tattered Tales is a game that requires players to compete as teams to create stories using a collection of random items to solve a problem.

To play a round, players will split into teams and each team is dealt five item cards.  A single situation card is revealed for all teams.  Players have two minutes to work together in teams to create a story that uses the items to resolve the situation.

Once the time is up, teams take turns telling their stories to the rest of the players.  Everybody votes on which story they liked the best (besides their own) and a new round begins with new teams.

After a few rounds, final scoring is calculated and a single player is given the title of Master Storyteller.

It’s the Time Tyrant’s wedding and they’ve called on you to whip up the feast! The Tyrant knows a chef is only
as good as their ingredients and has loaned you a time machine to let you leap through the ages and collect delicacies that will truly wow the guests.

Each round has three phases: 1) travel in time, 2) claim ingredients, 3) cleanup. Starting with the first player and proceeding clockwise, each player on their turn places a pawn on an era board, representing their claim on an ingredient from that era. Then, after everyone has placed their pawns, all chefs collect their ingredients, and the era boards are refilled.

Chefs are looking to show off their skills by featuring either a course of the same dish type (drink, appetizer, entree, or dessert) but of different eras known as a "Dish Set", or showcase the foods of one era known as an "Era Set".

The game ends immediately when either of the following happens during the “refill” step of the round’s Cleanup phase: either  3 or more era boards have all their cuisine spots filled with cards from eras other than their own (Paradoxes Aplenty), or it is not possible to fill all the cuisine spots on 1 or more era boards (Continuity Error). The chef with the tastiest tableau is the winner.

-description from publisher

Arena: The Contest – Legendary Box contains all stretch goals from the initial Kickstarter campaign for Arena: The Contest as well as all expansions from the first campaign (Heroes & Dungeons, Harun the Shapeshifter, Hydra, Ascaran the Archangel & Vanarus the Demon).

Kickstarter exclusive that includes 10 art cards and 8 award cards (plus a rules cards).

The art cards in the mini expansion feature two new icons that are not in the base game. The bonus ribbon gives you straight victory points, but doesn't help you meet any scoring conditions. The x2 icon doubles the icon in the slot that it points to.

Each game you can play with a different Award card that has a unique game end scoring rule. These cards add variability and complexity to make the game more replayable, especially at expert levels.

Card art descriptions:
"…Desires" (human teeth with rainbow gums at top and bottom)
"…Dread" (curly, leafless blue tree on the right)
"Scorched…" (purple/blue dragon in the upper left)
"…Expression" (street artist kneeling on the right spraying red into the center)
"Forgotten…" (unstable and overgrown tower ruin on the left)
"Simple…" (red, yellow, and blue paper airplanes at the top)
"…Imagination" (person riding unicorn with green wings in the upper right)
"Secret…" (open treasure chest in lower left with glowing blue contents)
"…Mirage" (an orange/purple rock formation seems to be starting to levitate)
"Pointless…" (green, red, and purple fireworks in the center)

A trivia game about Mexico. The board consists of a map of Mexico, with a route leading from the Northwest to the Southeast. The active player rolls a d6 which determines a trivia category, then has to answer a question in that category. If successful, they move forward; if wrong, others get a chance to answer the question. Movement range is decided by whether additional clues were used. Some fields also have special functions. The first player to arrive at the end of the route is the winner.

Make your collection complete with all the Sagrada promos released through the beginning of 2020! This pack contains the promos we've given at conventions, through reviewer partners, one released in Poland only and a promo exclusive to this pack!

description:
El Rebaño is not a usual card game... You’ll have to go around the table while bleating like a sheep, howl out loud while the rest of the players hide or be the shepherd that calls out the flock, so all the sheep go running towards him… It sounds crazy, right?

The aim of the game is to get rid of your cards. You play a card from your stack in the center of the table and then the turn will follow depending on the card played: Is it a coloured sheep card? The player with that colour will play the next card. Is it a special card like the wolf, the shepherd or the dog? The players will have to perform the action... It might even get dark and nobody will know which colour is for a while! Stay alert in the pen, don’t make any mistake, or you’ll get all the cards from the center of the table!

El Rebaño is an active, fast reaction card game for the whole flock at home!


El Rebaño no es un juego de cartas como los demás... ¿En qué juego tienes que dar vueltas a una mesa balando como una oveja? ¿O dar un largo aullido mientras los demás se esconden? ¿O que tenga turnos salteados? Por no decir que tienes que hacer de pastor, irte de la mesa y llamar al resto de las ovejas para que acudan corriendo hacia ti. ¿Te parecen suficientes razones para probar "El Rebaño"?

Cada jugador tendrá que deshacerse de su montón de cartas de la mano antes que los demás. Las cartas se juegan de una en una sobre la mesa, pero los turnos van saltando, ya que el siguiente jugador en jugar una carta será el jugador que sea del color de la oveja jugada. Pero a veces en vez de ovejas salen perros pastores (que inician duelos), lobos (que aullan), flores (para comérselas), el rpopio pastor (que llama al rebaño yéndose de la mesa de juego) y hasta se hace de noche (no sabiendo durante un rato que color eres). El primer jugador en quedarse sin cartas ganará la partida.
family: Organizations: Board Games Association of Creators in Spain (LUDO)

boardgame: Bag of Dungeon: Legends Character Expansion Pack
description:
New 2nd Edition! Upgrade your game! for use with any Bag of Dungeon title

The Legends expansion introduces new characters and game features to enhance your Bag of Dungeon adventures and deepen your play. Venture forth as Umari, wizard of the temporal arts. Strike from the shadows as Firkin the bold halfling. Make your presence known as the mighty minotaur, Grakun. Or play your own game as Esra, the lone monster hunter.

Plus: Upgrade your Bag of Dungeon characters with new instant power-ups and innate abilities, and expect the unexpected with new encounter cards: a random event every time you meet a monster.

CONTENTS: 4 new Bag of Dungeon character cards, 8 destiny cards, Umari’s spell card and 4 spell tokens, 1 instant skill reference card, 20 encounter cards, 23 skill/destiny tokens, 2 no-hand tokens, and a ‘How to Play’ booklet.

The game was developed in cooperation with professional firefighters and it includes realistic situations and educational content.

Fire Brigade is a cooperative game. Players are members of the fire brigade. Their goal is to save citizens by rescuing and transporting them from endangered areas to the hospital. At the same time, they want to prevent buildings from burning down and keep the fire contained by preventing the spread of a raging inferno. This game can be played in two modes. In Normal Mode players try to save as many citizens as they can, while in Expert Mode they must save all of the citizens or they lose the game.

The board consists of hexes with different areas (grass, forest, wetlands etc.) with drier areas having more chances to catch on fire. Buildings, Citizens and Fire Tokens are placed on the board according to the chosen scenario. There are nine scenarios for different number of players and difficulties.

Each player gets a Squad Card along with the matching Fire Truck Miniature and Starting Hex and is assigned one random Firefighting Ability Card and one random Utility Ability Card. That gives each Squad unique abilities to achieve the given goals easier.

The game has multiple rounds, each following the same procedure. Each round is divided into three phases: Fire-spreading Phase, Event Phase and Players’ Actions Phase.

In the Fire-spreading Phase the first player rolls the two Fire Dice. The sum of both numbers determines which hexes will be additionally affected by fire this turn. The fire intensifies and spreads according to the dice roll. If the fire intensifies too much, the Fire Tokens are replaced with an Inferno Token. Buildings on those hexes have burned to the ground and the Citizens are lost.

Then comes the Event Phase which changes the situation on the board. Events can be positive, negative or both.  There is an interesting fact about firemen and fire at the bottom of each event card.

The last phase is the Players’ Actions Phase. When it is their turn, each player can perform any combination of three basic actions (i.e. has three action points) and any number of free actions. The basic actions are: move Fire truck, extinguish fire, use Firefighting ability, use Utility ability, repair the Fire Truck, and evacuate Citizens. The free actions are: drop off Citizens in the Hospital and get Motivation Card, use Motivation card, drop off Citizens temporarily and pick them up after being dropped off. Players can pass at any point of their turn.

At the End of the game, players can check how well they did. Points are awarded based on how well the fire was contained and how many buildings were burned down. The score is then compared to the Score table which places players in categories from one to five stars, with stars being lost for every lost Citizen. By calculating their score, the players see how well they did and take a scenario with higher or lower difficulty, or try to beat their score in the same scenario next time.

—description from the publisher

description:
You are and evil Necromancer, searching the mouldering crypts and dusty tombs for decomposed cadavers, unholy spirits and demonic fiends to arise and do your infernal bidding. But you're not alone in this dark and forgotten place; a rival of equal evil awaits...

Necromancy: the Exhumation is a micro-card-game reminiscent of Magic: the Gathering but simpler in scale and scope.

The game is played by 2-players using a shared 20-card draw deck. Each card serves one of two purposes: as a creature or as a spell. On each turn players draw a card and take up to two separate actions: drawing or playing cards as creatures or spells. Each card has different attributes and effects and these are used to "attack" the other player and reduce their health points to zero.

boardgame: Purrballs: Catventure Card Game – Secret Store Expansion Pack
description:
"Many rumors exist among the chatty Catventurers, but none are as persistent as that of a mysterious store. Some say it moves from place to place, sometimes within the blink of an eye. Others whisper about the cloaked figure seated behind the counter. But all agree on its goods, powerful and valuable beyond compare, with a price tag to match..."

The Secret Store is the first expansion pack for the Purrballs: Catventure Card Game. It introduces Coins, a valuable resource that counts as XP (experience points) at the end of the game. Gain Coins by cashing in Bounties, trading in your valuable Loot cards for a fat purseful. Gather enough, and you can then purrchase Store cards; their powerful abilities are sure to help when you most need it!

—description from the publisher
This promo pack adds three new villager cards to your Shadowrift Game. The Guide, the bartender, and the Kidnapper.

boardgame: Cricaré: The Indian Holocaust – Wargame in Brazilian Colonial Era
description:
The Battle of Cricaré was one of the bloodiest massacres carried out by the Portuguese against Tupinambá - Tupinikín Indians. It happened on the São Mateus River, also known as Cricaré, in the until then Povoado do Cricaré, including on the Mariricu River (variation of the word Marerike, which means fortress of pau-a-pique), in the current state of Espírito Santo, at the beginning of the year 1558.

Fernão de Sá, son of Mem de Sá governor-general of Brazil at the time, when he arrived in Porto Seguro in Bahia, he received the information that there were many Indians in the region of the village of Cricaré. He set up a punitive expedition with about six caravels and 200 men. He disembarked on the banks of the Cricaré and began to slaughter the Gentiles, using their firearms against the ill-armed Indians.

By underestimating the bravery of the Indians and the carelessness of the soldiers, the governor’s son went a long way in the persecution and ran out of powder. The Indians realized and advanced on him that he was only with ten men. He was killed along with Manuel Álvares and Diogo Álvares, both son of Diogo Álvares Correia, The Caramuru - The man of fire - and three other soldiers.

Despite this victory by the Indians, a large number of Portuguese soldiers joined the Cricaré River region, killing thousands of Indians. However, two notable facts occurred: the first defeat of the Portuguese on the Brazilian coast and, in return, the greatest genocide committed against the Indians in Brazil.

The Bellum Brasilis series was originally produced by the extinct Riachuelo Games and was directed to games at the tactical level, covering several episodes in the history of Brazil. 
This wargame is the second in the Voxelhouse Old School Wargames series.

—description from the publisher

The Spanish-American War of 1898 was decided at sea, where both navies, with their shortcomings and strengths, gave their best.

Cuba & Filipinas is the expansion of Mar Rojo that covers this forgotten conflict. To play Cuba & Filipinas a copy of Mar Rojo is essential.

What does the Cuba & Filipinas expansion include:


     A deck of 50 new fleet cards, including all the important ships of the conflict, and others, that in different circumstances could have participated (such as the battleship Pelayo or the armored cruiser Maine).



     Two new action cards: RAM and DYNAMITE.



     A four-page mini rulebook with two new scenarios and the possibility of DYO (design your own) scenarios.

description:
Join the global community and manage your resources as you build your nation’s Economy, Global Engagement, Military, Human Progress, and Satisfaction. Can you lead your country to an age of happiness and prosperity?

REACTOR is a light, quick playing civilization building strategy game commissioned by NSquare and designed for use in the classroom to create teachable moments highlighting the dangers of nuclear weapons.

Gameplay
Each turn consists of 3 phases in this order: Infrastructure, Action, and Aftermath

Infrastructure Phase
Starting with the first player, everyone chooses one category to raise up 2 levels.

Action Phase
Each player secretly chooses which action they will take by playing the corresponding card face down in front of themselves. There are 5 possible actions. Economic Progress: Add resources to any category. Global Responsibility: Create an alliance and/or generate resources. Human Achievement: Add resources to your lowest category. Satisfaction: Convert military resources to any other category and get bonus resources. Military: Invade or Nuke another nation. All actions are explained in greater detail below.

Once all players have locked in a choice, the cards are revealed, and the actions are resolved starting with the first player.

Aftermath Phase
Players with an active alliance automatically produce additional resources which can be placed anywhere. Allied players receive 1 resource per alliance at level 2, 2 resources per alliance at level 5, and 3 resources per alliance at level 10.

Then the first player marker is moved clockwise from the current first player and a new round begins.

End

At the end of the game, any nations with a total score of 30 or more points from all categories, except Military, (or with a Satisfaction level of 8+ and total score of 25 or more points) are considered winners unless all remaining nations have winning totals in which case the player with the lowest Satisfaction score loses. If players have a tie for lowest Satisfaction score then break that tie with their point total from the other three categories.

If all nations have been Nuked, then everyone loses.

If only 1 Nation remains, that nation is technically “the winner” but they are now the sole survivors of a nuclear wasteland.

Among younger demographics, the nuclear issue is abstract and distant—a problem of previous generations. There are more resonant issues like social injustice, global warming, or economy disparity that, while still daunting, are far more comprehensible and consequential.
Reactor, engages high school students in the classroom about the nuclear weapons threat by connecting this issue to more visible social matters through an interactive and accessible medium. By breaking down the complexity of this problem with play, students cultivate a sense of understanding that drives engagement, helping foster new generations that feel empowered and responsible to take action.

About NSquare: 
In 2014, five of the largest peace and security funders in the United States joined forces to develop a new, collective approach to incentivizing and rewarding innovation in the nuclear threat field. What emerged was N Square, a path-breaking initiative built on the idea that new forms of cross-sector collaboration—combined with the sheer ingenuity of an engaged public­—will accelerate the achievement of internationally agreed goals to reduce nuclear dangers.

—description from the designer

boardgame: Connect with Pieces: Star Trek Into Darkness
description:
Show your friends who’s the ultimate puzzler in the new Star Trek Connect With Pieces Puzzle Building Game! Each puzzle piece has one of 5 symbols on it (they are cleverly pressed in so that they are only visible when looking for them). Score points by finding the right pieces! Match up more symbols for more points! Featuring 2 modes of play, you can ramp up the speed or the strategy for different play styles. Featuring a proprietary manufacturing method. The primary image is maintained while the game play is layered in for a unique take on this classic activity.

Features & details

     Great Collectable.
     Official Licensed Product.
     Each puzzle piece has a symbol embossed into the piece-match symbols to score points.
     Clever design of the puzzle allows for some pieces to be placed in multiple locations.
     700 piece puzzle game.


—description from the publisher

Take on the role of a Japanese police officer as you find evidence and solve crimes! You can eat ramen to boost your energy and execute special moves, and when you find the criminal, draw their face!

This game takes place on a roll of Washi tape, and is thus endlessly modular and scales up to any player count.

—description from the publisher

One player (Woody Woodpecker) starts at the red center space. The other players start at the corners of their color. Movement is determined by a die. Woody Woodpecker may move in any direction, the hunters may only move forward. They are only allowed to use the blue dots if they are on a rectangular blue space and roll a 1 or a 6. If a hunter player catches Woody Woodpecker, he gets a chip and  changes the roles with the Woody Woodpecker player. Woody Woodpecker continues to play, while the hunters have to roll a 6 to continue their hunt.
The player who has the most chips after an agreed on amount of time wins the game.

—user summary

In PizzaGross you are running a shabby pizza shop, trying to convince your customers that your product is edible enough to justify paying for it. Try to save as much money as possible on ingredients, while keeping the customers happy with their purchases.

PizzaGross is a card drafting game with an economic component. Players must collect sets of ingredient cards to create pizzas of varying qualities. After drafting the ingredient cards, players choose which of them they shall buy. The cheapest ingredients are obtained for no money at all, but higher quality cards require some investment. When selling pizzas, players will face customers of different levels of rigorousness. Rotten ingredients may satisfy some customers, but others will notify the authorities, who will close the business for the day.

After three rounds, the player with the most money is the winner.

-description from designer

boardgame: The Drums (fan expansion for T.I.M.E Stories)
description:
This fan-made scenario for T.I.M.E Stories is a single player expansion. It tells a tale of atmospheric horror in the woods of Kentucky. It is set in 1974.

—description from the designer

SkirmishCampaigns:Russia 43- Red Guards at Kurskis a complete book for recreating skirmish-level battles and campaigns centered on the Soviet 11th Guards Army and its thrust on the northern flank of the Kursk salient in July 1943. This is the second in a set of two books (the first is Gross Deutschland at Kursk) focused on the titanic struggle around Kursk.
Maximum Replayability: the SkirmishCampaigns system features a realistic variable order of battle for each scenario, guaranteeing countless unique scenario and campaign replays.

Generic Order of Battle: this book also includes a unique generic order of battle listing that can be translated to almost any skirmish rule system.

Historical Research: this book includes several introduction sections that contain background information on the German offensive at Kursk in 1943.

Includes:

     10 Scenarios,
     3 Campaigns


Compatible with these and other Skirmish Rules: Battleground, Beer and Pretzels Skirmish (BAPS), ARC of FIRE, Face of Battle, Men of Frost, Overlord, Battalions in Crisis, Battlefront WWII, Cross of Iron, And in the Beginning, Skirmish '90, and more.

—description from the publisher

This game was attached to the ioGioco 7 magazine.

The evil boss Karl Blueyes organizes illegal futuristic motorcycle races every Saturday night, thus fueling his profitable round of illegal business. In the improvised arena that uses city streets, the champions of the two most feared urban gangs, the Wheels of Judgment and the Gods of Atomic Speed, face off. During the race, the two drivers try to put the opposing motorcycle at gunpoint using the light tracer. Who at the end of the race will have scored more tracers on the other bike will be the winner!

To move his motorcycle, the player plays one of his numbered cards. At the end of the turn, a tracer can also play in the space provided. If a player plays a card to move, the value of which is equal to that of a tracer already played, this tracer is activated and the motorcycle behind (to understand who it is, you need to determine the shorter arc that separates the two motorcycles) can shoot, taking by the tracer card. On the tracer cards there are the symbols of the hits, whoever has collected the most hits at the end of the race wins.

—user summary

This game was attached to the ioGioco 8 magazine.

The race has just ended but some fraudulent directors are not interested in telling the truth. Everyone is in the pay of a different team and tries, with still images, forward and rewind, to mount a summary of the race that gives greater visibility to the team for which he works in secret.

The game board represents the last section of the circuit, and is divided into squares. The cars are three-dimensional and are moved in turn by each player following precise rules (the machines move one or two squares only orthogonally and pushing each other), five times before determining the end of the lap. Thanks to special cards ("Freeze", "Skip" and "Prev & Play") it is possible to influence the sequence of movements.

—user summary

boardgame: Ferro, Fango e Neve: L'operazione Barbarossa in 50 minuti
description:
Ferro, Fango e Neve is a World War II wargame that was attached to issue 9 of the ioGioco Italian game magazine.

On June 22, 1941 Hitler unleashes Operation Barbarossa and surprisingly invades the Soviet Union. It is a struggle against time: the Germans must bring the USSR to its knees before Stalin regroups and goes to the counterattack.

—user summary

description:
This game was attached to issue 11 of the ioGioco magazine.

The forest is full of juicy berries and fragrant fruits. Each animal, sniffing the air, listening to the rustle of the leaves and calculating the best routes, tries to reach the richest bushes. But other gluttons target the same succulent fruits. We must hurry, before someone faster blows us under the nose, or better to say under the muzzle, our favorite acorns or the red strawberries.

Each turn you draw a bush tile characterized by a value (its position in the woods) and two mathematical operations. Then you turn a "track" piece, which indicates numerical values corresponding to colored positions. The player tries to use the numerical values together with the operations, putting them in the right order on his board to reach a number as close as possible to that of the bush.

—user summary

Darkball is a first of its kind game in which players put their timing to the test against a speedy disappearing ball and each other. Darkball rewards accuracy, reaction times, as well as creativity. As the pieces are rearranged, the ball can travel in any direction, and down any path with no limits to the number of players or ways it can be played.

Any endpoint Blink displaying cyan has a Darkball. Play begins when one player presses their endpoint Blink to release the Darkball. The ball moves along the pathway, making decisions randomly when it reaches forks. Clustered pathways are treated as forks, and can produce very chaotic behavior.

When the Darkball reaches your endpoint, you must press the button to bounce it back into the pathway. If you click too early, your endpoint will go dark for a moment, and you cannot press again until it brightens. If you press too late, you will take damage, as indicated by red lights on your endpoint. You can then release the Darkball by pressing.

Once a player has missed the Darkball 6 times, their endpoint will explode, and they are out. The last player remaining is the winner.

This game was attached to issue 13 of the ioGioco magazine.

Year 2811: Earth is a dead planet and the world population has sought survival in colonies outside the world. After a series of bloody wars to dominate the solar system, fought with huge battle mechs, the colonies have lived in precarious peace for decades. The memory of the last Great War remains alive and is celebrated with a tournament that takes place annually on the surface of the devastated planet Earth: the Mecha Brawl.
Mercenaries, ex-soldiers and adventurers challenge each other on board their mechs for glory and money, led by generals hired by the Colonies. You are one of them, and you have been called to manage the team that will represent your colony in the next tournament: choose two teams of mercenaries, deploy your team and prepare for battle!

The Quick Start Rules are a highly simplified set of tactical combat rules for starship combat based on the Solar Starfire ruleset. Written in three sections, this 11-page rulebook contains the basic tactical rules, six scenarios, and the printable counters and map required for the scenarios. Th Quick Start Rules are designed for players new to the Starfire System and introduce all of the basic concepts of the Starfire tactical game.

—description from the designer

boardgame: The Fight: We gonna fight them all!
description:
Welcome to The Fight!

You and your fellow crew have heard of an abandoned dungeon with rumors of great treasures inside, yet nobody else but you has dared to check the truth about these rumors — and now you know why: A horde of monsters inside the dungeon got bored and now they crave a good fight.

There is no time for a long strategy session. Use the weapons at hand, making the right combined decisions of how to fight against the various monsters without revealing too much information to them as blind trust in your crew may be the best chance you have to coordinate your strengths.

The Fight is played in fifty levels. Each level takes only a few minutes, with you fighting against a certain number of monsters. Typically, one monster lies face up in the center of play, and your crew needs to beat its strength value in one attack to win the fight. For each attack, you all place one card from your hand face down in front of you. If your revealed fighting total matches or exceeds the value of a face-up monster, you beat it; otherwise, the monster remains face up and keeps fighting.

To win a level, you can discuss your strategy with your crew. You can never talk about your card values in hand, but can debate whether you want to "strike fast" or "take it slow". You can also use available special actions to gain the most benefits from them.

—description from the publisher

7 Fortunes is a 2 - 6 Player Battle Arena Board Game, based upon the rich content and stories of Greek Mythology.

     Players pick from a pool of 35 Champions
     Participate in a “Stacking” Mini Card Game in which they gain Wealth and Hero Points.
     Use their Champion Abilities, Aquire & Collect Items, and Battle Other Players!
     All while Progressing to a Target Victory Point Count or the Elimination of all the other players for the win!


—description from the designer

boardgame: A Death in the Red Light
description:
Amsterdam, 1988. The Red Light District is a hive of sex and drugs. You take the role of a Police Detective. A dead body has turned up on the streets. You must examine the clues, follow the leads and solve the puzzles in order to uncover the truth of what happened in this real historical case.

In a combination choose-your-adventure and puzzle game, there are multiple paths you can take to solving the mystery. Play alone or with friends. The game can be played online or printed out at home.

The Noir graphic style and jazz soundtrack work together to immerse you in the grimy police work of the Red Light District.

—description from the designer

Glass Bead is a territory game for two players: Red and Blue. It is played on the spaces of an initially empty square board. Recommended sizes are between 7x7 and 10x10 spaces.

An empty space is controlled by the player with more stones adjacent to it. Diagonal adjacencies also count. A space occupied by a stone is controlled by the owner of that stone.

Red plays first, then turns alternate. On your turn, place a stone of your color on a empty space that is not controlled by either player.

The game ends when no more placements are possible. The player who controls more spaces in the final position wins.

According to the designer, this game, later reworked into Kuzushi, suffers from a huge first-player advantage. It is suggested to use komi (compensation points for the second player) to solve this issue.

The magic number "7" conjures up meanings, powers, and  coincidences dating back to the dawn of time. In Quatorze, you establish seven columns that utilize the powers of these powerful 7s. Players take turns adding cards to these columns, while trying to retain high-value cards in their hand. When the game ends, the highest combined value of cards in hand wins.

However, the powers of cards played to the columns during the game can drastically change the way cards are scored, and each player is trying to maneuver these powers to their advantage! Can you best harness and use these powerful 7s?

Quatorze is a sequel to Jason Tagmire's Seven7s and includes the original game within it as well. Thus, instead of seven suits, the game now includes fourteen!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: Guards and Martyrs: The Battle for Basra, December 1986-April 1987
description:
"Guards and Martyrs: The Battle for Basra, December 1986-April 1987" is a two-player Operational level board-wargame of one of the largest and bloodiest battle of the whole Iran-Iraq War.

“The battle for Basra will be the mother of all battles.” –Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani to the Iranian Supreme Defense Council, January 23, 1987.

The Iran-Iraq War was starting its seventh year by the start of Winter in 1986. Casualties on both sides numbered in the hundreds of thousands and both sides had resorted to chemical weapons, missile attacks against their opponent’s cities, and an escalating air and sea campaign in the Persian Gulf that threatened to bring Super power intervention. The Iranian government felt that an offensive to take the crucial city of Basra, Iraq’s only port and primary gateway for shipping out its oil and receiving goods from abroad, could end the war with a victory. Dubbed “Karbala 5” the Iranians amassed much of their Revolutionary Guard formations supported by new weapons secretly purchased from the “Great Satan” USA (via the illegal Iran-Contra affair) as well as North Korea. A feint attack north of the battlefield, Karbala 6, by much of the Iranian armored force would tie up Iraq’s reserves. The resulting battle for Basra was one of the largest and bloodiest battles fought in the war, with both sides fighting with all they had. In the end the battle for Basra would prove just as indecisive as the larger war, with neither side able to claim a clear victory.

Can you do as well or better than your historical counterparts? Learn and enjoy!

—description from the publisher

boardgame: To Rule the Waves: Naval Battles in the Mediterranean in the 1880s.
description:
“The surer of himself an admiral is, the finer the tactical development of his fleet, the better his captains, the more reluctant must he necessarily be to enter into a melee with equal forces, in which all these advantages will be thrown away, chance reign supreme, and his fleet be place on terms of equality with an assemblage of ships which have never before acted together.”—Alfred Thayer Mahan, The Influence of Seapower Upon History, 1660-1783.

To Rule The Waves is an introductory level wargame simulation on the British Navy’s bombardment of the Egyptian Forts at Alexandria in 1882, as well as several “what if” naval battles that could have occurred amongst the European powers in the Mediterranean during the 1880s. This decade was marked by peace amongst the Imperialist nations, but there were events that “rocked the boat” and could very easily have led to conflict; especially in the Mediterranean Sea that for ages has been the nexus of commerce as well as conflict. This decade was also marked by tremendous innovation in warship design and tactics. The pace of change was such that no nation could rest assured that their fleet’s composition was proven or superior to that of their opponent. This was not an easy time for Admirals and ships’ captains…

The game design is meant to be simple and highly playable, yet one that portrays the historical challenges facing the combatants.

Learn and enjoy!

—description from the publisher

In Universal Rule: Singularity, players battle for control of the universe in this pocket-sized 4X style game. You can colonize new planets, explore new areas on those planets, gain credits based off of the resources that the planet produces, or attempt to destroy other planets that threaten your existence.
